tools.description: Create a new GitHub issue for tracking bugs, feature requests, or tasks. Use this for actionable work items that need assignment, labeling, and status tracking. For reports, announcements, or status updates that don't require task tracking, use create_discussion instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 1 issue(s) can be created. Labels ["test-reliability"] will be automatically added.

body.description: Detailed issue description in Markdown. Do NOT repeat the title as a heading since it already appears as the issue's h1. Include context, reproduction steps, or acceptance criteria as appropriate.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

labels.description: Labels to categorize the issue (e.g., 'bug', 'enhancement'). Labels must exist in the repository.

parent.description: Parent issue number for creating sub-issues. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 42 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/42). Can also be a temporary_id (e.g., 'aw_abc123', 'aw_Test123') from a previously created issue in the same workflow run.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

temporary_id.description: Unique temporary identifier for referencing this issue before it's created. Format: 'aw_' followed by 3 to 12 alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'aw_abc1', 'aw_Test123'). Use '#aw_ID' in body text to reference other issues by their temporary_id; these are replaced with actual issue numbers after creation.

title.description: Concise issue title summarizing the bug, feature, or task. The title appears as the main heading, so keep it brief and descriptive.

tools.name: create_issue

tools.description: Report that a tool or capability needed to complete the task is not available, or share any information you deem important about missing functionality or limitations. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because the required functionality is missing or access is restricted.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this tool is needed or what information you want to share about the limitation (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tool.description: Optional: Name or description of the missing tool or capability (max 128 characters). Be specific about what functionality is needed.

tools.name: missing_tool

tools.description: Log a transparency message when no significant actions are needed. Use this to confirm workflow completion and provide visibility when analysis is complete but no changes or outputs are required (e.g., 'No issues found', 'All checks passed'). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

message.description: Status or completion message to log. Should explain what was analyzed and the outcome (e.g., 'Code review complete - no issues found', 'Analysis complete - all tests passing').

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: noop

tools.description: Report that data or information needed to complete the task is not available. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because required data, context, or information is missing.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

context.description: Additional context about the missing data or where it should come from (max 256 characters).

data_type.description: Type or description of the missing data or information (max 128 characters). Be specific about what data is needed.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this data is needed to complete the task (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: missing_data

tools.description: Signal that the task could not be completed due to an infrastructure or tool failure (e.g., MCP server crash, missing authentication, inaccessible repository). Use this when required tools or data are unavailable and the task cannot be meaningfully performed. This is distinct from noop (no action needed) — it indicates an active failure that prevented the task from running. The workflow framework will treat this as a failure signal even when the agent exits successfully.

details.description: Optional extended details or diagnostic context about the failure (max 65000 characters).

reason.description: A concise explanation of why the task could not be completed (max 1024 characters). Be specific about which tools or resources were unavailable.

tools.name: report_incomplete

tools.description: Get information on a specific pull request in GitHub repository.

method.description:
Action to specify what pull request data needs to be retrieved from GitHub. 
Possible options: 
 1. get - Get details of a specific pull request.
 2. get_diff - Get the diff of a pull request.
 3. get_status - Get combined commit status of a head commit in a pull request.
 4. get_files - Get the list of files changed in a pull request. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.
 5. get_review_comments - Get review threads on a pull request. Each thread contains logically grouped review comments made on the same code location during pull request reviews. Returns threads with metadata (isResolved, isOutdated, isCollapsed) and their associated comments. Use cursor-based pagination (perPage, after) to control results.
 6. get_reviews - Get the reviews on a pull request. When asked for review comments, use get_review_comments method.
 7. get_comments - Get comments on a pull request. Use this if user doesn't specifically want review comments. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.
 8. get_check_runs - Get check runs for the head commit of a pull request. Check runs are the individual CI/CD jobs and checks that run on the PR.

{"number":37864,"title":"Tracking: Known pre-existing CI test failures","body":"## Purpose

This issue tracks **known, pre-existing CI test failures** that are caused by infrastructure, service-side, or external dependency issues — not by code bugs in this repository. The automated [Analyze CI Test Failures](.github/workflows/fix-test-failures.md) workflow uses this issue to skip known failures and avoid filing duplicate issues.

## Known Failures

_No known CI (playback) failures at this time._

> **Note:** This list only tracks failures from **CI pipelines** (`ci.yml`, playback mode). Live-test pipeline failures (`tests.yml`) are excluded — the workflow filters them out by check-run name.

## Maintenance Guidelines

- **Adding entries:** When a CI failure is confirmed to be a pre-existing infrastructure/service issue (not a code bug), add it here with the package name, test pattern, error summary, root cause, and a tracking issue link.
- **Removing entries:** When the underlying issue is fixed and CI is green again, remove the entry and close the corresponding tracking issue.
- **Format:** Each entry must include the **package name** (as it appears in check-run names) so the workflow can match it.
- **Review cadence:** Review this list monthly to check if any known failures have been resolved.

## Release Preparation

Sets release date to **2026-04-22** for both packages and finalizes
changelogs.

### @azure/core-amqp 4.4.2
- **Bug fix**: Handle `AggregateError` in `translate()` for Node.js 20+
Happy Eyeballs (#38233)
- **Bug fix**: Clamp negative `setTimeout` values to 0 for Node.js v24+
(#38166)

### @azure/event-hubs 6.0.4
- **Bug fix**: Clamp negative timeout computations to 0 for Node.js v24+
(#38166)
- **Perf**: Event-driven queue signals, simplified serialization,
optimized cloning (#37882)

---------

## Changes

### New test files
- base64.spec.ts (node + browser)
- interfaceHelpers.spec.ts
- operationHelpers.spec.ts
- pipeline.spec.ts
- utils.spec.ts
- urlHelpers.spec.ts (expanded)

### Expanded existing tests
- deserializationPolicy.spec.ts: error paths, XML parsing, header
deserialization
- serializationPolicy.spec.ts: body serialization edge cases, stream
bodies
- serializer.spec.ts: polymorphism, flattening, readonly, date/bytearray
edge cases
- serviceClient.spec.ts: auth, custom pipelines, error handling, base
URI
- authorizeRequestOnClaimChallenge.spec.ts: challenge parsing edge cases
- authorizeRequestOnTenantChallenge.spec.ts: multi-call token flows

### Test quality improvements
- Replaced mutable request capture with vi.fn() pattern
- Replaced try/catch + assert.fail with rejects.toThrow() /
toMatchObject()
- Replaced calledOnce flags with vi.fn().mockImplementationOnce()
- deepEqual on primitives changed to strictEqual
- assert.equal(x, undefined) changed to assert.isUndefined(x)

---------

{"number":38247,"title":"[ContentUnderstanding] Fix 1.1.0 user agent version string","body":"### Packages impacted by this PR
@azure/ai-content-understanding

### Issues associated with this PR
None

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR
The custom user agent string in contentUnderstandingContext.ts was hardcoded as azsdk-js-ai-content-understanding/1.0.1 incorrectly. It should 1.1.0.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR chosen?
Updated the hardcoded version string to 1.1.0 to match package.json.

### Are there test cases added in this PR? _(If not, why?)_
No. This is a one-line string literal change. The existing test suite covers client creation and pipeline behavior.

### Provide a list of related PRs _(if any)_
None

### Command used to generate this PR:**_(Applicable only to SDK release request PRs)_
None

### Checklists
- [x] Added impacted package name to the issue description
- [ ] Does this PR needs any fixes in the SDK Generator?** _(If so, create an Issue in the [Autorest/typescript](https://github.com/Azure/autorest.typescript) repository and link it here)_
- [ ] Added a changelog (if necessary)

{"number":38247,"title":"[ContentUnderstanding] Fix 1.1.0 user agent version string","body":"### Packages impacted by this PR
@azure/ai-content-understanding

### Issues associated with this PR
None

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR
The custom user agent string in contentUnderstandingContext.ts was hardcoded as azsdk-js-ai-content-understanding/1.0.1 incorrectly. It should 1.1.0.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR chosen?
Updated the hardcoded version string to 1.1.0 to match package.json.

### Are there test cases added in this PR? _(If not, why?)_
No. This is a one-line string literal change. The existing test suite covers client creation and pipeline behavior.

### Provide a list of related PRs _(if any)_
None

### Command used to generate this PR:**_(Applicable only to SDK release request PRs)_
None

### Checklists
- [x] Added impacted package name to the issue description
- [ ] Does this PR needs any fixes in the SDK Generator?** _(If so, create an Issue in the [Autorest/typescript](https://github.com/Azure/autorest.typescript) repository and link it here)_
- [ ] Added a changelog (if necessary)

{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"Improve test coverage to ~100% for @azure/core-client (#38176)

## Changes

### New test files
- base64.spec.ts (node + browser)
- interfaceHelpers.spec.ts
- operationHelpers.spec.ts
- pipeline.spec.ts
- utils.spec.ts
- urlHelpers.spec.ts (expanded)

### Expanded existing tests
- deserializationPolicy.spec.ts: error paths, XML parsing, header
deserialization
- serializationPolicy.spec.ts: body serialization edge cases, stream
bodies
- serializer.spec.ts: polymorphism, flattening, readonly, date/bytearray
edge cases
- serviceClient.spec.ts: auth, custom pipelines, error handling, base
URI
- authorizeRequestOnClaimChallenge.spec.ts: challenge parsing edge cases
- authorizeRequestOnTenantChallenge.spec.ts: multi-call token flows

### Test quality improvements
- Replaced mutable request capture with vi.fn() pattern
- Replaced try/catch + assert.fail with rejects.toThrow() /
toMatchObject()
- Replaced calledOnce flags with vi.fn().mockImplementationOnce()
- deepEqual on primitives changed to strictEqual
- assert.equal(x, undefined) changed to assert.isUndefined(x)

---------

"}},"created_at":"2026-04-23T10:20:36Z","updated_at":"2026-04-23T10:24:50Z"},{"number":38259,"title":"feat(confidential-ledger): add redirect URL caching for write operations","body":"### Packages impacted by this PR

`@azure-rest/confidential-ledger`

### Issues associated with this PR

Related to [ADO Task #37485929](https://msazure.visualstudio.com/One/_workitems/edit/37485929) (parent Feature #37442109)

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

When writing to Azure Confidential Ledger, the load balancer redirects write requests (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) to the primary node via a 307 redirect. Every write pays this redirect cost, adding 3-10 seconds of latency per request. This is especially painful for batch workloads that do many sequential writes.

This PR caches the redirect target URL after the first 307/308 redirect so that subsequent writes go directly to the primary node, bringing write latency down to around 230ms.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR chosen?

The design mirrors Ryan Zhang's Python SDK implementation (PR #46254 in azure-sdk-for-python). A closure-based cache variable holds the redirect target URL within the existing confidentialLedgerRedirectPolicy. This was chosen because:

- It keeps cache state per-client (no shared global state between clients)
- It builds on the existing custom redirect policy added in PR #37249
- It matches the behavior of the Python SDK so users get a consistent experience across languages

Cache behavior:
- Write requests (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) that receive a 307/308 cache the target and reuse it on subsequent writes
- Read requests (GET/HEAD) never use or populate the cache
- Server errors (5xx) or transport errors on writes invalidate the cache so the next write goes back through the load balancer
- 303 POST-to-GET conversions do not cache (the method changed, so the target is not guaranteed to be the primary)

### Are there test cases added in this PR?

Yes. 18 new unit tests covering cache population, cache bypass for reads, cache invalidation on errors, and edge cases (308 support, URL restore on transport errors, 303 conversion handling, redirect-follow failures). All 30 tests pass (12 existing + 18 new).

Also live tested against a real ledger (ryan-sdk-acl). Round 1 showed the expected 307 redirect on the first write, and Round 2 showed 0 redirects with 5 direct writes at around 230ms each.

### Provide a list of related PRs

- Ryan Zhang's auth-preservation PR (merged): #37249
- Ryan Zhang's Python SDK caching (reference implementation): [azure-sdk-for-python#46254](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/pull/46254)

"}},"created_at":"2026-04-23T01:00:51Z","updated_at":"2026-04-23T01:13:38Z"},{"number":38256,"title":"feat(warp): add tsgo compiler mode using TypeScript 7 native CLI","body":"## Summary

Add a new `--compiler tsgo` flag to warp that uses the native Go-based TypeScript compiler (`tsgo`) from `@typescript/native-preview` for ~10x faster builds.

## What's changed

### New: `--compiler tsgo` CLI flag
When `--compiler tsgo` is passed, warp uses the `tsgo` CLI binary instead of the TypeScript programmatic API for compilation. The default (`tsc`) is unchanged.

### Compilation strategy (4 paths)
1. **Identical emit identity** → copy output from already-compiled sibling (dedup)
2. **CJS from ESM sibling** → derive via esbuild transform (`buildTransformedOutput`)  
3. **Has polyfills** → fall back to TS programmatic API (needs custom CompilerHost)
4. **Default** → compile with `tsgo` CLI

### Files changed
- **`common/tools/warp/src/tsgoCompiler.ts`** (new) — Core tsgo backend: binary resolution, single-target compilation, multi-target orchestration with dedup/fallback
- **`common/tools/warp/src/build.ts`** — Added `compiler` field to `BuildOptions`, tsgo branch in `compileStep()`
- **`common/tools/warp/src/cli.ts`** — Added `--compiler` flag with validation and help text
- **`common/tools/warp/src/diagnostics.ts`** — `formatDiagnostics()` now handles `diagnosticText` for backend-agnostic output
- **`common/tools/warp/package.json`** — Added `@typescript/native-preview` as a direct dependency
- **`common/tools/warp/test/public/tsgoCompiler.spec.ts`** (new) — 11 tests covering resolution, version, compilation, fallback, dedup, and integration

### Test results
All 283 tests pass (272 existing + 11 new). Type-check clean. Lint clean.

## Usage
```bash
warp --compiler tsgo          # use tsgo for compilation
warp --compiler tsc           # use default TS API (unchanged)
warp                          # default: tsc
```

## Why draft?
- TypeScript 7.0 is still in beta (`@typescript/native-preview`)
- No programmatic API until 7.1 — polyfill targets still fall back to TS API
- Waiting for broader team feedback on the approach

"}},"created_at":"2026-04-22T19:56:01Z","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:27:09Z"},{"number":38245,"title":"Add breaking-change analysis agent for ARM SDK migrations","body":"## What this PR does

Adds a new Copilot agent (\mgmt-breaking-change-analysis\) that analyzes breaking changes in ARM management SDK PRs migrating from Swagger/AutoRest to TypeSpec/emitter.

### Files

- **\.github/agents/mgmt-breaking-change-analysis.agent.md\** -- Agent definition with mandatory execution protocol, phase skeleton, state tracking, cascade detection checklist, pattern matching step, and validation rules
- **\.github/prompts/mgmt-breaking-change-analysis-guidelines.md\** -- Detailed workflow instructions covering context gathering, CHANGELOG extraction, root cause investigation (with cascade detection algorithm), four-layer swagger comparison model, sub-agent delegation, and report templates
- **\.github/prompts/mgmt-breaking-change-patterns.md\** -- Lookup table of 10 architect-approved breaking change patterns for the HLC-to-Modular migration (sourced from autorest.typescript issues)

### How it works

The agent follows a structured multi-phase workflow:

1. **Phase 1: Gather Context** -- Extract PR metadata, build per-service API version map, locate four spec layers (old swagger, new swagger pre-TypeSpec, TypeSpec-generated swagger, SDK api.md)
2. **Phase 2: Extract Breaking Changes** -- Parse CHANGELOG entries from PR branch
3. **Phase 3: Root Cause Analysis** -- Investigate model-level changes first, signature entries last; build broken_models set and type dependency graph; detect cascading breakings through nested type references
4. **Phase 4: Pattern Matching** -- Match Type 2 (migration-related) root causes against architect-approved patterns to pre-fill the Accepted column
5. **Phase 5: Verification and Report** -- Verify all entries classified (Type 1 + Type 2 = total), build structured report

### Key design decisions

- **Agent as control plane**: The agent file defines mandatory execution steps and validation rules. The guidelines file serves as the knowledge base with full procedural details. This separation ensures the agent always follows the workflow.
- **Explicit state tracking**: The agent externalizes intermediate results (broken_models, type_dependency_graph, classified_entries) after each phase to maintain consistency across long analysis chains.
- **Cascade detection checklist**: Every 'new signature' entry requires documenting 4 checks (direct params, options changes, cascade from broken models, emitter differences) to prevent premature attribution.

### Scope

- ARM management SDK packages migrating from Swagger/AutoRest to TypeSpec/emitter only
- Not applicable to data-plane SDKs, RLC packages, or hand-written SDKs

### Examples:

{"number":38259,"title":"feat(confidential-ledger): add redirect URL caching for write operations","body":"### Packages impacted by this PR

`@azure-rest/confidential-ledger`

### Issues associated with this PR

Related to [ADO Task #37485929](https://msazure.visualstudio.com/One/_workitems/edit/37485929) (parent Feature #37442109)

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

When writing to Azure Confidential Ledger, the load balancer redirects write requests (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) to the primary node via a 307 redirect. Every write pays this redirect cost, adding 3-10 seconds of latency per request. This is especially painful for batch workloads that do many sequential writes.

This PR caches the redirect target URL after the first 307/308 redirect so that subsequent writes go directly to the primary node, bringing write latency down to around 230ms.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR chosen?

The design mirrors Ryan Zhang's Python SDK implementation (PR #46254 in azure-sdk-for-python). A closure-based cache variable holds the redirect target URL within the existing confidentialLedgerRedirectPolicy. This was chosen because:

- It keeps cache state per-client (no shared global state between clients)
- It builds on the existing custom redirect policy added in PR #37249
- It matches the behavior of the Python SDK so users get a consistent experience across languages

Cache behavior:
- Write requests (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) that receive a 307/308 cache the target and reuse it on subsequent writes
- Read requests (GET/HEAD) never use or populate the cache
- Server errors (5xx) or transport errors on writes invalidate the cache so the next write goes back through the load balancer
- 303 POST-to-GET conversions do not cache (the method changed, so the target is not guaranteed to be the primary)

### Are there test cases added in this PR?

Yes. 18 new unit tests covering cache population, cache bypass for reads, cache invalidation on errors, and edge cases (308 support, URL restore on transport errors, 303 conversion handling, redirect-follow failures). All 30 tests pass (12 existing + 18 new).

Also live tested against a real ledger (ryan-sdk-acl). Round 1 showed the expected 307 redirect on the first write, and Round 2 showed 0 redirects with 5 direct writes at around 230ms each.

### Provide a list of related PRs

- Ryan Zhang's auth-preservation PR (merged): #37249
- Ryan Zhang's Python SDK caching (reference implementation): [azure-sdk-for-python#46254](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/pull/46254)

### Checklists
- [x] Added impacted package name to the issue description
- [ ] Does this PR needs any fixes in the SDK Generator? No
- [x] Added a changelog (if necessary)","state":"open","draft":false,"merged":false,"mergeable_state":"blocked","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/pull/38259","user":{"login":"Tewatia5355","id":60055574,"profile_url":"https://github.com/Tewatia5355","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60055574?v=4"},"labels":["Confidential Ledger"],"requested_reviewers":["taicchoumsft","ryazhang-microsoft"],"head":{"ref":"yakumar/confidentialLedger/redirect_caching","sha":"[REDACTED]","repo":{"full_name":"Azure/azure-sdk-for-js","description":"This repository is for active development of the Azure SDK for JavaScript (NodeJS & Browser). {"number":38256,"title":"feat(warp): add tsgo compiler mode using TypeScript 7 native CLI","body":"## Summary

Add a new `--compiler tsgo` flag to warp that uses the native Go-based TypeScript compiler (`tsgo`) from `@typescript/native-preview` for ~10x faster builds.

## What's changed

### New: `--compiler tsgo` CLI flag
When `--compiler tsgo` is passed, warp uses the `tsgo` CLI binary instead of the TypeScript programmatic API for compilation. The default (`tsc`) is unchanged.

### Compilation strategy (4 paths)
1. **Identical emit identity** → copy output from already-compiled sibling (dedup)
2. **CJS from ESM sibling** → derive via esbuild transform (`buildTransformedOutput`)  
3. **Has polyfills** → fall back to TS programmatic API (needs custom CompilerHost)
4. **Default** → compile with `tsgo` CLI

### Files changed
- **`common/tools/warp/src/tsgoCompiler.ts`** (new) — Core tsgo backend: binary resolution, single-target compilation, multi-target orchestration with dedup/fallback
- **`common/tools/warp/src/build.ts`** — Added `compiler` field to `BuildOptions`, tsgo branch in `compileStep()`
- **`common/tools/warp/src/cli.ts`** — Added `--compiler` flag with validation and help text
- **`common/tools/warp/src/diagnostics.ts`** — `formatDiagnostics()` now handles `diagnosticText` for backend-agnostic output
- **`common/tools/warp/package.json`** — Added `@typescript/native-preview` as a direct dependency
- **`common/tools/warp/test/public/tsgoCompiler.spec.ts`** (new) — 11 tests covering resolution, version, compilation, fallback, dedup, and integration

### Test results
All 283 tests pass (272 existing + 11 new). Type-check clean. Lint clean.

## Usage
```bash
warp --compiler tsgo          # use tsgo for compilation
warp --compiler tsc           # use default TS API (unchanged)
warp                          # default: tsc
```

## Why draft?
- TypeScript 7.0 is still in beta (`@typescript/native-preview`)
- No programmatic API until 7.1 — polyfill targets still fall back to TS API
- Waiting for broader team feedback on the approach

This approach avoids suppressing linter errors. 

This helper might fit better in `core-util`.

{"number":38245,"title":"Add breaking-change analysis agent for ARM SDK migrations","body":"## What this PR does

Adds a new Copilot agent (\mgmt-breaking-change-analysis\) that analyzes breaking changes in ARM management SDK PRs migrating from Swagger/AutoRest to TypeSpec/emitter.

### Files

- **\.github/agents/mgmt-breaking-change-analysis.agent.md\** -- Agent definition with mandatory execution protocol, phase skeleton, state tracking, cascade detection checklist, pattern matching step, and validation rules
- **\.github/prompts/mgmt-breaking-change-analysis-guidelines.md\** -- Detailed workflow instructions covering context gathering, CHANGELOG extraction, root cause investigation (with cascade detection algorithm), four-layer swagger comparison model, sub-agent delegation, and report templates
- **\.github/prompts/mgmt-breaking-change-patterns.md\** -- Lookup table of 10 architect-approved breaking change patterns for the HLC-to-Modular migration (sourced from autorest.typescript issues)

### How it works

The agent follows a structured multi-phase workflow:

1. **Phase 1: Gather Context** -- Extract PR metadata, build per-service API version map, locate four spec layers (old swagger, new swagger pre-TypeSpec, TypeSpec-generated swagger, SDK api.md)
2. **Phase 2: Extract Breaking Changes** -- Parse CHANGELOG entries from PR branch
3. **Phase 3: Root Cause Analysis** -- Investigate model-level changes first, signature entries last; build broken_models set and type dependency graph; detect cascading breakings through nested type references
4. **Phase 4: Pattern Matching** -- Match Type 2 (migration-related) root causes against architect-approved patterns to pre-fill the Accepted column
5. **Phase 5: Verification and Report** -- Verify all entries classified (Type 1 + Type 2 = total), build structured report

### Key design decisions

- **Agent as control plane**: The agent file defines mandatory execution steps and validation rules. The guidelines file serves as the knowledge base with full procedural details. This separation ensures the agent always follows the workflow.
- **Explicit state tracking**: The agent externalizes intermediate results (broken_models, type_dependency_graph, classified_entries) after each phase to maintain consistency across long analysis chains.
- **Cascade detection checklist**: Every 'new signature' entry requires documenting 4 checks (direct params, options changes, cascade from broken models, emitter differences) to prevent premature attribution.

### Scope

- ARM management SDK packages migrating from Swagger/AutoRest to TypeSpec/emitter only
- Not applicable to data-plane SDKs, RLC packages, or hand-written SDKs

### Examples:

{"number":38242,"title":"Add setup script and skills for Azure AI Content Understanding","body":"This pull request introduces comprehensive documentation and workflow improvements for running and understanding JavaScript SDK samples for Azure AI Content Understanding. It adds detailed skill guides, usage instructions, troubleshooting steps, and helper scripts to streamline the developer experience, especially for new users setting up or running SDK samples.

**Key changes include:**

### Skill Documentation & Guidance

- Added `cu-sdk-common-knowledge` skill documentation, providing domain knowledge, official documentation links, and copilot guidance for answering questions about Azure AI Content Understanding concepts, analyzers, API operations, and SDK usage.
- Added `cu-sdk-js-sample-run` skill documentation, detailing interactive workflows for running JavaScript SDK samples, prerequisites, environment setup, sample selection, and troubleshooting. Includes step-by-step user prompts for a guided experience.

### Scripts & Sample Execution

- Added a `README.md` in the `scripts` directory, documenting the usage and workflow of `setup_samples.sh` and `run_single_sample.sh` scripts, manual setup alternatives, troubleshooting, and directory structure for running JavaScript samples.

These updates make it significantly easier to onboard, set up, and troubleshoot Azure AI Content Understanding SDK samples, while providing clear guidance and references for both the SDK and the underlying service.","state":"open","draft":true,"merged":false,"mergeable_state":"unknown","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/pull/38242","user":{"login":"changjian-wang","id":15209050,"profile_url":"https://github.com/changjian-wang","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/15209050?v=4"},"labels":["Cognitive - Content Understanding"],"head":{"ref":"changjian-wang/cu-sdk-skills","sha":"[REDACTED]","repo":{"full_name":"Azure/azure-sdk-for-js","description":"This repository is for active development of the Azure SDK for JavaScript (NodeJS & Browser). **What changed:** One character in the reference link on line 1358.

**How I found it:** A customer reported broken doc links via OCV after migrating to the new Foundry portal. The NPS auto-analysis confirmed the issue came from REST API doc links. I audited all azure-sdk-for-* repos and this was the only one affected. {"number":38259,"title":"feat(confidential-ledger): add redirect URL caching for write operations","body":"### Packages impacted by this PR

`@azure-rest/confidential-ledger`

### Issues associated with this PR

Related to [ADO Task #37485929](https://msazure.visualstudio.com/One/_workitems/edit/37485929) (parent Feature #37442109)

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

When writing to Azure Confidential Ledger, the load balancer redirects write requests (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) to the primary node via a 307 redirect. Every write pays this redirect cost, adding 3-10 seconds of latency per request. This is especially painful for batch workloads that do many sequential writes.

This PR caches the redirect target URL after the first 307/308 redirect so that subsequent writes go directly to the primary node, bringing write latency down to around 230ms.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR chosen?

The design mirrors Ryan Zhang's Python SDK implementation (PR #46254 in azure-sdk-for-python). A closure-based cache variable holds the redirect target URL within the existing confidentialLedgerRedirectPolicy. This was chosen because:

- It keeps cache state per-client (no shared global state between clients)
- It builds on the existing custom redirect policy added in PR #37249
- It matches the behavior of the Python SDK so users get a consistent experience across languages

Cache behavior:
- Write requests (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) that receive a 307/308 cache the target and reuse it on subsequent writes
- Read requests (GET/HEAD) never use or populate the cache
- Server errors (5xx) or transport errors on writes invalidate the cache so the next write goes back through the load balancer
- 303 POST-to-GET conversions do not cache (the method changed, so the target is not guaranteed to be the primary)

### Are there test cases added in this PR?

Yes. 18 new unit tests covering cache population, cache bypass for reads, cache invalidation on errors, and edge cases (308 support, URL restore on transport errors, 303 conversion handling, redirect-follow failures). All 30 tests pass (12 existing + 18 new).

Also live tested against a real ledger (ryan-sdk-acl). Round 1 showed the expected 307 redirect on the first write, and Round 2 showed 0 redirects with 5 direct writes at around 230ms each.

### Provide a list of related PRs

- Ryan Zhang's auth-preservation PR (merged): #37249
- Ryan Zhang's Python SDK caching (reference implementation): [azure-sdk-for-python#46254](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/pull/46254)

### Checklists
- [x] Added impacted package name to the issue description
- [ ] Does this PR needs any fixes in the SDK Generator? No
- [x] Added a changelog (if necessary)","state":"open","draft":false,"merged":false,"mergeable_state":"blocked","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/pull/38259","user":{"login":"Tewatia5355","id":60055574,"profile_url":"https://github.com/Tewatia5355","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60055574?v=4"},"labels":["Confidential Ledger"],"requested_reviewers":["taicchoumsft","ryazhang-microsoft"],"head":{"ref":"yakumar/confidentialLedger/redirect_caching","sha":"[REDACTED]","repo":{"full_name":"Azure/azure-sdk-for-js","description":"This repository is for active development of the Azure SDK for JavaScript (NodeJS & Browser). {"number":38259,"title":"feat(confidential-ledger): add redirect URL caching for write operations","body":"### Packages impacted by this PR

`@azure-rest/confidential-ledger`

### Issues associated with this PR

Related to [ADO Task #37485929](https://msazure.visualstudio.com/One/_workitems/edit/37485929) (parent Feature #37442109)

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

When writing to Azure Confidential Ledger, the load balancer redirects write requests (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) to the primary node via a 307 redirect. Every write pays this redirect cost, adding 3-10 seconds of latency per request. This is especially painful for batch workloads that do many sequential writes.

This PR caches the redirect target URL after the first 307/308 redirect so that subsequent writes go directly to the primary node, bringing write latency down to around 230ms.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR chosen?

The design mirrors Ryan Zhang's Python SDK implementation (PR #46254 in azure-sdk-for-python). A closure-based cache variable holds the redirect target URL within the existing confidentialLedgerRedirectPolicy. This was chosen because:

- It keeps cache state per-client (no shared global state between clients)
- It builds on the existing custom redirect policy added in PR #37249
- It matches the behavior of the Python SDK so users get a consistent experience across languages

Cache behavior:
- Write requests (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) that receive a 307/308 cache the target and reuse it on subsequent writes
- Read requests (GET/HEAD) never use or populate the cache
- Server errors (5xx) or transport errors on writes invalidate the cache so the next write goes back through the load balancer
- 303 POST-to-GET conversions do not cache (the method changed, so the target is not guaranteed to be the primary)

### Are there test cases added in this PR?

Yes. 18 new unit tests covering cache population, cache bypass for reads, cache invalidation on errors, and edge cases (308 support, URL restore on transport errors, 303 conversion handling, redirect-follow failures). All 30 tests pass (12 existing + 18 new).

Also live tested against a real ledger (ryan-sdk-acl). Round 1 showed the expected 307 redirect on the first write, and Round 2 showed 0 redirects with 5 direct writes at around 230ms each.

### Provide a list of related PRs

- Ryan Zhang's auth-preservation PR (merged): #37249
- Ryan Zhang's Python SDK caching (reference implementation): [azure-sdk-for-python#46254](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/pull/46254)

### Checklists
- [x] Added impacted package name to the issue description
- [ ] Does this PR needs any fixes in the SDK Generator? No
- [x] Added a changelog (if necessary)","state":"open","draft":false,"merged":false,"mergeable_state":"blocked","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/pull/38259","user":{"login":"Tewatia5355","id":60055574,"profile_url":"https://github.com/Tewatia5355","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/60055574?v=4"},"labels":["Confidential Ledger"],"requested_reviewers":["taicchoumsft","ryazhang-microsoft"],"head":{"ref":"yakumar/confidentialLedger/redirect_caching","sha":"[REDACTED]","repo":{"full_name":"Azure/azure-sdk-for-js","description":"This repository is for active development of the Azure SDK for JavaScript (NodeJS & Browser). - serviceClient.spec.ts: auth, custom pipelines, error handling, base URI
- authorizeRequestOnClaimChallenge.spec.ts: challenge parsing edge cases
- authorizeRequestOnTenantChallenge.spec.ts: multi-call token flows

- Replaced mutable request capture with vi.fn() pattern
- Replaced try/catch + assert.fail with rejects.toThrow() / toMatchObject()
- Replaced calledOnce flags with vi.fn().mockImplementationOnce()
- deepEqual on primitives changed to strictEqual

[{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"[ContentUnderstanding] Fix 1.1.0 user agent version string (#38247)","author":{"name":"Chien Yuan Chang","email":"ds.chienyuanchang@gmail.com","date":"2026-04-23T01:36:35Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-23T01:36:35Z"}},"author":{"login":"chienyuanchang","id":186005646,"profile_url":"https://github.com/chienyuanchang","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/186005646?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"Prepare release: @azure/core-amqp 4.4.2 and @azure/event-hubs 6.0.4 (#38246)

## Release Preparation

Sets release date to **2026-04-22** for both packages and finalizes
changelogs.

### @azure/core-amqp 4.4.2
- **Bug fix**: Handle `AggregateError` in `translate()` for Node.js 20+
Happy Eyeballs (#38233)
- **Bug fix**: Clamp negative `setTimeout` values to 0 for Node.js v24+
(#38166)

### @azure/event-hubs 6.0.4
- **Bug fix**: Clamp negative timeout computations to 0 for Node.js v24+
(#38166)
- **Perf**: Event-driven queue signals, simplified serialization,
optimized cloning (#37882)

## Summary

Fixes `TimeoutNegativeWarning` raised by Node.js v24+ when `setTimeout`
receives a negative timeout value.

#38163

## Root Cause

In `sdk/eventhub/event-hubs/src/withAuth.ts`, a deadline pattern
computes remaining time as `timeoutAfterStartTime - Date.now()`. If
prior steps (opening CBS session, acquiring lock) consume more time than
the original timeout budget, this goes **negative**. The negative value
flows into `setTimeout()` in `requestResponseLink.sendRequest()` and
`lock.acquire()`, triggering:

```
TimeoutNegativeWarning: -1 is a negative number.
Timeout duration was set to 1.
```

This is cosmetic — Node clamps to 1ms and the operation succeeds — but
the warning triggers ops alerts.

The same pattern exists in Service Bus's `linkEntity.ts`.

## Fix

Clamp timeout values to `Math.max(..., 0)` at all affected sites
(defense in depth):

| File | Sites | Change |
|------|-------|--------|
| `sdk/eventhub/event-hubs/src/withAuth.ts` | 4 | Clamp
`timeoutAfterStartTime - Date.now()` |
| `sdk/servicebus/service-bus/src/core/linkEntity.ts` | 3 | Clamp
`timeoutInMs - (Date.now() - startTime)` |
| `sdk/core/core-amqp/src/requestResponseLink.ts` | 1 | Clamp
`timeoutInMs` in `sendRequest()` |
| `sdk/core/core-amqp/src/util/lock.ts` | 1 | Clamp `timeoutInMs` in
`acquire()`'s `setTimeout` |

Co-authored-by: Byron Jin <byronjin@microsoft.com>","author":{"name":"Azure SDK Bot","email":"53356347+azure-sdk@users.noreply.github.com","date":"2026-04-22T01:06:09Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-22T01:06:09Z"}},"author":{"login":"azure-sdk","id":53356347,"profile_url":"https://github.com/azure-sdk","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/53356347?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"Handle AggregateError in core-amqp translate() for Node.js 20+ Happy Eyeballs (#38233)

### Packages impacted by this PR

- `@azure/core-amqp`
- `@azure/event-hubs` (tests only)

### Issues associated with this PR

- Fixes #38231
- Supersedes #38190

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

Node.js 20+ defaults to Happy Eyeballs (RFC 8305), racing IPv4/IPv6
connections. DNS failures now throw `AggregateError` instead of a single
system error. `translate()` in core-amqp didn't handle this, returning
raw untranslated errors — breaking `instanceof MessagingError` checks,
`.retryable` logic, and error code handling for all consumers.

### Fix

`translate()` now maps `AggregateError` → new `AggregateError` with all
inner errors translated to `MessagingError`, with `.retryable` stamped
on the aggregate (true if any inner is retryable).

### Are there test cases added in this PR?

Yes — 6 new tests in `core-amqp` covering ENOTFOUND, EAI_AGAIN, mixed
retryability, nested aggregates, and empty aggregates. Event Hubs test
helper updated to unwrap `AggregateError`.

## Package
@azure/cosmos

## Summary

Fixed incorrect SQL filter generation in ORDER BY queries with
continuation tokens. Backslashes and single quotes in `orderByItem`
values are now properly escaped in `formatValueForSQL` before being
embedded in WHERE clauses.

## Root Cause

`formatValueForSQL` embeds continuation token values directly into SQL
WHERE clauses without escaping. Backslash sequences like `\u2013`, `\n`,
`\t` are interpreted by the Cosmos DB SQL parser as unicode escapes or
control characters instead of literal text, causing filter mismatches
that silently skip rows.

## Fix

In `formatValueForSQL`, escape values before embedding in SQL string
literals:

1. **Backslashes:** `\` → `\\` — prevents parser from interpreting
`\uXXXX`, `\n`, `\t` etc. as escape sequences
2. **Single quotes:** `'` → `\u0027` (unicode escape) — prevents string
delimiter confusion

### Why `\u0027` instead of `''` for quotes?

Traditional `''` doubling is ambiguous after `\\`. For value `it\'s`,
after escaping: `'it\\''s'` — parser sees `\\` as literal backslash,
then `'` as end of string (not a `''` pair). `\u0027` is unambiguous in
all contexts.

Backslashes are escaped first, then quotes, so literal `\u0027` in data
becomes `\\u0027` — distinguishable from our injected `\u0027`.

## Testing

Unit tests added covering backslash escaping, quote escaping,
backslash-quote interaction, unicode sequences, and edge cases.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <175728472+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>","author":{"name":"Azure SDK Bot","email":"53356347+azure-sdk@users.noreply.github.com","date":"2026-04-18T17:12:04Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-18T17:12:04Z"}},"author":{"login":"azure-sdk","id":53356347,"profile_url":"https://github.com/azure-sdk","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/53356347?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"chore(core): remove dead code, unnecessary casts, and redundant async (#38149)

## Summary

Remove dead code, unnecessary type casts, and redundant `async` keywords
across core packages.

### Dead code removal

- `AsyncLockOptions` interface, `Timeout` class (core-amqp)
- `isBearerToken`, `isPopToken` helpers (core-auth)
- `getCachedDefaultHttpsClient`, unused `HttpClient` import
(core-client-rest)
- Stale `declare global { interface Event {} }` (abort-controller)

### Cast removal / type narrowing

- `as any` → `as ParsedOutput` in `parseConnectionString` (core-amqp)
- `{ [k: string]: string }` → `Record` (core-amqp)
- `as string` → `?? ""` for CAE challenge header (core-rest-pipeline)
- `isKeyCredential` param `any` → `unknown` with proper guard
(core-client-rest)
- `objectHasProperty` tightened with null check (core-util)

### Redundant async removal

- `sendRequest`, `sendPolicy` handlers that just return a promise
without awaiting (core-client, core-client-rest, core-http-compat,
core-lro)

### Simplify defensive logic

- **ndJsonPolicy**: Remove unreachable `Array.isArray` guard after
`JSON.parse` on a string starting with `[` (JSON spec guarantees array
result)
- **tokenCycler**: Replace optional chaining with explicit null guard in
`shouldRefresh`, making intent clearer

### Other improvements

- `Resolved` simplified to `Awaited` with deprecation notice
(core-tracing)
- `HttpHeaders` class made non-exported (core-http-compat, was already
not in public API)
- `Set` for passthrough props lookup (core-http-compat)
- XML root key extraction simplified (core-xml)
- URL scope construction fix (core-client)
- Optional chaining removed on guaranteed field (core-paging)

### Packages impacted by this PR

`@azure/ai-projects`

### Issues associated with this PR


### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

Release candidate refresh for `@azure/ai-projects` v2.0.2 RC1, updating
the TypeSpec input commit and regenerating client surface area (new
operations + helpers), plus wiring CI for the `sdk/ai` service
directory.

Also fixes a bug in `createFilePartDescriptor`
(`src/static-helpers/multipartHelpers.ts`) where a truthy check `if
(fileInput.contents)` would incorrectly handle cases where `contents` is
an empty string `""`, causing the entire wrapper object to be serialized
as the multipart body instead of just the `contents` value.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If
there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR
chosen?

- **Truthy check (previous)**: `if (fileInput.contents)` — fails for
falsy values like empty string `""`.
- **Existence/type guard (chosen)**: `typeof fileInput === "object" &&
fileInput !== null && "contents" in fileInput` — correctly identifies
the wrapper object shape regardless of whether `contents` is falsy.

The existence/type guard approach was chosen because it accurately
distinguishes between the two input shapes (`FileContents` vs. wrapper
object) without relying on the truthiness of the `contents` value.

Additionally, the parameter and return types were tightened from `any`
to well-defined types (`FileContents | { contents: FileContents;
contentType?: string; filename?: string }` and a new
`FilePartDescriptor` interface respectively) for improved type safety.

### Are there test cases added in this PR? _(If not, why?)_

No new tests added for the multipart helper fix. The change is a
targeted correctness fix to the type guard logic; the existing multipart
serialization is covered by the broader code-based agent operation
tests.

Sync eng/common directory with azure-sdk-tools for PR","author":{"name":"Azure SDK Bot","email":"53356347+azure-sdk@users.noreply.github.com","date":"2026-04-16T18:24:14Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-16T18:24:14Z"}},"author":{"login":"azure-sdk","id":53356347,"profile_url":"https://github.com/azure-sdk","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/53356347?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"Add issue-triage gh-aw workflow (#38153)

## What
Port the intelligent issue triage workflow from
[azure-sdk-for-net](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/.github/workflows/issue-triage.md)
to this repository.

## Why
Automate initial issue triage to reduce manual effort — classify issues,
predict labels, look up owners, and route to the right team members.

## What the workflow does
1. **Retrieve & validate** — fetches new issue, skips if already labeled
2. **Customer evaluation** — classifies author as team member, customer,
or bot using `author_association`
3. **Label prediction** — predicts category (`Client`/`Mgmt`) and
service labels from issue content
4. **Deprecated package check** — queries npm registry; auto-closes
issues for fully deprecated packages
5. **Owner lookup** — scans `CODEOWNERS` `ServiceLabel` entries
bottom-to-top, assigns owners
6. **Routing comment** — pings owners via `mention_owners` step
7. **Analysis comment** — posts structured triage summary with label
confidence and routing details

## Adaptations from .NET
- npm registry instead of NuGet for deprecation checks
- JS category labels (`Client`, `Mgmt`, `Central-EngSys`, `Mgmt-EngSys`,
`Service`)
- Package naming (`@azure/storage-blob` format)
- CODEOWNERS examples updated for JS repo structure
- Created `bot` label for bot-filed issue classification
- Compiled with gh-aw v0.68.3

---------

## Problem

`createFile` and `encodeByteArray` silently corrupt data when given a
`Uint8Array` subarray (via `.subarray()`). Both read from the full
backing `ArrayBuffer` instead of the subarray range.

## Fix

- **`createFile`** (`core-rest-pipeline`): copy subarrays that do not
span the full buffer
- **`encodeByteArray`** (`core-client`): pass `byteOffset`/`byteLength`
to `Buffer.from()`

Tests added for both.

## Summary

Improve cross-platform test compatibility across core packages by moving
Node-specific tests to `node/` subdirectories and replacing `Buffer`
usage with platform-agnostic alternatives.

### Node-specific test isolation

Moved tests that depend on Node.js APIs to `node/` subdirs:
- `core-rest-pipeline`: formDataPolicy, multipartPolicy,
wrapAbortSignalLike
- `ts-http-runtime`: formDataPolicy

### Buffer removal in cross-platform tests

Replaced `Buffer.from`/`Buffer.concat` with `Uint8Array`/`TextEncoder`
in test helpers for `core-client`, `core-rest-pipeline`, and
`core-amqp`.

### New tests

- `core-xml`: 5 XML round-trip tests
- `core-rest-pipeline`: expanded wrapAbortSignalLike coverage,
getHeaderName test

### Minor

- Cast removal in `wrapAbortSignal.ts` (`as any` → `in` check)
- Type-safe mock with `satisfies PipelineResponse`
- `resolve(undefined)` for type correctness

Per #38115, the documentation folder has four inconsistencies that the
docs-consistency-check workflow flagged but couldn't auto-PR (it lacks
PR-creation permission).

## Changes

### `using-azure-identity.md` — DefaultAzureCredential chain was
incomplete
Listed only `EnvironmentCredential` and `ManagedIdentityCredential` for
the chain. The actual implementation in
`sdk/identity/identity/src/credentials/defaultAzureCredential.ts` tries
**eight** credentials in order. Added the missing six
(`WorkloadIdentityCredential`, `VisualStudioCodeCredential`,
`AzureCliCredential`, `AzurePowerShellCredential`,
`AzureDeveloperCliCredential`, `BrokerCredential`).

### `writing-performance-tests.md` — non-existent `tsconfig.package`
reference
The example `tsconfig.json` for a perf test project extended
`tsconfig.package`, which doesn't exist at the repo root. The actual
template perf test extends `tsconfig.lib.json`.

### `resolve-pnpm-lock-merge-conflict.md` — missing `/` in the `git add`
one-liner
The combined shell command was missing the path separator between the
shell-substituted repo root and `pnpm-lock.yaml`.

### `steps-after-generations.md` — missing `...` suffix on three `pnpm
build --filter` commands
The Linux record block already had `...` (so dependencies are built);
the other three (Windows record, Linux playback, Windows playback) were
inconsistent and have been fixed to match.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>","author":{"name":"Deyaa","email":"dealmaha@microsoft.com","date":"2026-04-14T20:42:22Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-14T20:42:22Z"}},"author":{"login":"deyaaeldeen","id":6074665,"profile_url":"https://github.com/deyaaeldeen","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6074665?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"chore(core): add react-native and @types/react devDependencies (#38135)

Add `react-native` and `@types/react` as devDependencies to all 14 core
packages. Required for type-checking the react-native build target.

## Additional fixes

**`tsconfig.browser.base.json`** — Exclude `src/**/*-react-native.*`
from browser test compilation. Without this, `tsc` picks up react-native
platform files (e.g. `runtimeInfo-react-native.mts`) which `import
"react-native"`, pulling in globals that conflict with DOM types.

**`core-amqp/src/util/utils.ts`** — Change `_timer` type from
`ReturnType<typeof setTimeout>` to `number | NodeJS.Timeout`. The root
`tsconfig.src.react-native.json` inherits `types: ["node"]` from
`tsconfig.src.build.json`, so both `@types/node` and react-native
globals are in scope. React-native overrides `setTimeout` to return
`number` while `@types/node` returns `NodeJS.Timeout`, and the local
`class Timeout` shadows the type name, causing a conflict. This is
resolved properly in the `#platform/*` migration where the react-native
tsconfig uses `types: ["react-native"]` only.

### `@azure/arm-kubernetesconfiguration`

**Failing tests:**
- `kubernetesconfiguration test > operation list test` in `sdk/kubernetesconfiguration/arm-kubernetesconfiguration/test/kubernetesconfiguration_operations_test.spec.ts`

**Check run:** `js - kubernetesconfiguration - mgmt (Build UnitTest macoslatest_24x_node)` → [run 70757322962](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/runs/70757322962)

**CI test summary:** Failed: 1 (1 new, 0 recurring) / Passed: 6 / Other: 12 / Total: 19

**Error:**
```
RecorderError: Start request failed.
 ❯ Recorder.start ../../test-utils/recorder/src/recorder.ts:336:19
 ❯ test/kubernetesconfiguration_operations_test.spec.ts:41:5

Serialized Error: { statusCode: undefined }
```

**Root cause:**

The `Recorder.start()` call in `beforeEach` POSTs to the test proxy to begin a playback session. The test proxy responded with a non-200 HTTP status (the status code is not propagated into the `RecorderError` constructor, so it surfaces as `statusCode: undefined`). This is a **test-infrastructure failure**, not a code or test-logic bug:

- The recording file **exists** in `Azure/azure-sdk-assets` at tag `js/kubernetesconfiguration/arm-kubernetesconfiguration_615562201f`, path `js/sdk/kubernetesconfiguration/arm-kubernetesconfiguration/recordings/node/kubernetesconfiguration_test/recording_operation_list_test.json`.
- The recording format and content are valid (single `GET /providers/Microsoft.KubernetesConfiguration/operations?api-version=2023-05-01` → 200 response with a `value` array of 27 operations).
- 6 other tests passed in the same CI run, confirming the test proxy **was** running for part of the pipeline.
- The failure is classified as **1 new, 0 recurring**, indicating it appeared for the first time on this specific CI run.
- The triggering commit (`41b10c38` / PR #38018) added `arm-kubernetesconfiguration-extensions` to `ci.mgmt.yml` for the first time, expanding the set of packages tested in a single CI run from 4 to 5.

The most likely explanation is a test proxy crash or resource-contention issue on the macOS Node 24 CI runner when managing asset-sync checkout across the newly expanded set of 5 packages. The test proxy must switch between asset tags (`arm-kubernetesconfiguration_615562201f` and the newly added `arm-kubernetesconfiguration-extensions_e78462a13b`) during the run, which may have caused a state issue.

**Suggested fix:**

No code change is required — the recording and source are correct. The recommended investigation steps are:

1. **Re-trigger the CI pipeline** on `main` to determine whether this is a transient failure. If it passes on a re-run, add the test to a flakiness watchlist.
2. **Check test proxy asset-sync behavior** when multiple packages share a service directory and have different asset tags. Specifically, verify the test proxy correctly isolates the working directory for each package's `assets.json` tag when running sequentially.
3. If the failure recurs, **check macOS Node 24 test proxy logs** in Azure DevOps build `#20260410.1` for the `Build UnitTest macoslatest_24x_node` job to see the exact HTTP status code returned by the test proxy to `recorder.start()`.

**Reproduced locally:** No (test proxy binary download fails in sandboxed network environment; the recording and source code are structurally correct)

---

## Additional Notes

**`js - openai (Build Build)` failure excluded:** The openai CI pipeline explicitly sets `RunUnitTests: false` (see `sdk/openai/ci.yml`). Its "Build Build" failure (`Manifest generation failed` / `Path does not exist: /mnt/vss/_work/_temp/packages-dev-publish`) is a CI packaging/dev-publish infrastructure failure, not a unit test failure, and is therefore out of scope for this issue.

test failures on main: `@azure/attestation`","body":"## Summary

1 package is affected by live test failures detected in CI on the `main` branch. The HEAD commit `[REDACTED]` (merged 2026-03-12) shows 2 failing check runs, both in the `js - attestation - tests` CI job. 1 test is failing out of 212 total. The failure is in a live-mode test (the attestation package runs against real Azure Attestation infrastructure, not in playback mode).

## Failures

### `@azure/attestation`

**Failing tests:**
- `[AAD] Attestation Client > #attestTpm` in `sdk/attestation/attestation/test/public/attestationTests.spec.ts`

**Error:**

```
A VBS attestation policy has not been set on the attestation provider.
```

Reported in check run `js - attestation - tests` (id: `66769152361`) and `js - attestation - tests (Public windows_20x_browser)` (id: `66768403727`) on commit `[REDACTED]`.

**Root cause:**

The `#attestTpm` test is a **live** test (as configured in `sdk/attestation/attestation/tests.yml` via the `archetype-sdk-tests.yml` pipeline template and `platform-matrix.json`). It runs against a real Azure Attestation service instance rather than using recorded HTTP playback.

The test first sets a TPM attestation policy on the service, then calls `client.attestTpm()`. The service is returning an error indicating "A VBS attestation policy has not been set on the attestation provider." This is a service-side infrastructure issue: the Azure Attestation provider instance used by CI does not have a VBS (Virtualization Based Security) attestation policy configured, which the service now requires before it will process TPM attestation requests.

The test code itself is:
```typescript
it("#attestTpm", async () => {
  const client = createRecordedClient(recorder, "AAD", true);
  const adminClient = createRecordedAdminClient(recorder, "AAD");

  // Set the policy on the instance to a known value.
  await adminClient.setPolicy(
    KnownAttestationType.Tpm,
    "version=1.0; authorizationrules{=> permit();}; issuancerules{};",
  );

  const encodedPayload = JSON.stringify({ payload: { type: "aikcert" } });
  const result = await client.attestTpm(encodedPayload);
  // ...
});
```

The test sets a `Tpm` policy successfully but the subsequent `attestTpm` call fails with the VBS policy error. This indicates a change in the Azure Attestation service behaviour: a VBS policy must now also be configured on the provider before TPM attestation can proceed.

**Suggested fix:**

This is **not a code bug** — it is a live-test infrastructure configuration issue. No source code change is needed. The fix requires one of the following:

1. **Configure VBS policy on the CI attestation provider**: The CI service principal that manages the attestation provider needs to set a VBS attestation policy (e.g., `version=1.0; authorizationrules{=> permit();}; issuancerules{};`) on the `Vbs` attestation type before the TPM test can succeed. This is done through the Azure portal or via `az attestation policy set`.

2. **Update the test to also set a VBS policy**: If VBS policy is now always required alongside TPM policy for this test scenario, update the test to also set (and then reset) a VBS policy before calling `attestTpm`:

```diff
--- a/sdk/attestation/attestation/test/public/attestationTests.spec.ts
+++ b/sdk/attestation/attestation/test/public/attestationTests.spec.ts
@@ -169,6 +169,10 @@ describe("[AAD] Attestation Client", () => {
     await adminClient.setPolicy(
       KnownAttestationType.Tpm,
       "version=1.0; authorizationrules{=> permit();}; issuancerules{};",
     );
+    await adminClient.setPolicy(
+      KnownAttestationType.Vbs,
+      "version=1.0; authorizationrules{=> permit();}; issuancerules{};",
+    );
 
     const encodedPayload = JSON.stringify({ payload: { type: "aikcert" } });
 
@@ -182,6 +186,7 @@ describe("[AAD] Attestation Client", () => {
 
     // Reset the policy on the instance to the default for reproducibility.
     await adminClient.resetPolicy(KnownAttestationType.Tpm);
+    await adminClient.resetPolicy(KnownAttestationType.Vbs);
   });
```

Note: Before adopting option 2, verify whether `KnownAttestationType.Vbs` exists in the SDK or needs to be added, and confirm with the attestation service team that VBS policy is now a prerequisite for TPM attestation. Option 1 (infrastructure fix) is preferred if the test environment can be updated.

**Reproduced locally:** No (live test — requires Azure Attestation service credentials and infrastructure with TPM hardware support)

---

## Additional Notes

- The attestation test suite runs exclusively in **live mode** against real Azure Attestation infrastructure (see `sdk/attestation/attestation/tests.yml`). There are recorded playback files for this test (`recording_attesttpm.json` in `Azure/azure-sdk-assets` tag `js/attestation/attestation_bc4c12556e`) that show all 4 requests returning HTTP 200, meaning a previous recording was successful. The current failure is due to a service-side behaviour change in the live environment.
- The HEAD commit (`[REDACTED]`) was an AutoPR for `@azure-arm-postgresql-flexible` — it has no relation to the attestation package. This confirms the failure is an **environmental/service configuration regression**, not a code regression from the merged PR.
- The browser test check run (`js - attestation - tests (Public windows_20x_browser)`) shows only a generic `PowerShell exited with code '1'` failure with no additional test-level annotation, suggesting it was aborted or hit the same service error.

test failures on main: communication-identity, storage-blob, communication-short-codes, documentintelligence, openai","body":"## Summary

5 packages have failing CI check runs on the HEAD commit of `main` ([`4adaf9f`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]), 2026-03-18). The failures are distributed across `@azure/communication-identity` (12 tests), `@azure/storage-blob` (9 tests — all new), `@azure/communication-short-codes` (7 tests), `@azure-rest/ai-document-intelligence` (build-step failure, 0 test failures), and `@azure/openai` (311 tests, 295 recurring). All failures originate from **live service or infrastructure issues**, not from logic regressions in the package source code itself.

---

## Failures

### `@azure/communication-identity`

**Failing tests (12, all recurring):**
- `Get Token For Teams User [Playback/Live] [AAD]` — multiple platforms
- `Get Token For Teams User [Playback/Live]` — multiple platforms

**Error:**
```
AADSTS5000224: We are sorry, this resource is not available.
If you are seeing this message by mistake, please contact Microsoft support.
Trace ID: ea12d67a-763d-407f-8bd2-85cbfcfb2a00
```

**Root cause:**
The Azure Active Directory tenant/resource used by the CI service principal for Teams-user token tests returns `AADSTS5000224`, indicating the associated AAD resource is unavailable (possibly deprovisioned or misconfigured in the test environment). This is a live-mode test that calls real Azure AD endpoints; the error is 100% service-side and **does not point to any code bug**. All 12 failures are "recurring," meaning the issue predates the current HEAD.

**Suggested fix:**
No code change is needed. The CI service principal's Teams-user resource must be re-provisioned or the test environment reconfigured by the Azure Communication Services team. Consider adding a `skipEnv` / `isLiveMode` guard in the test to skip gracefully when the resource is unavailable, rather than failing:

````diff
--- a/sdk/communication/communication-identity/test/...spec.ts
+++ b/sdk/communication/communication-identity/test/...spec.ts
@@ ...
-  it("Get Token For Teams User", async () => {
+  it("Get Token For Teams User", async function() {
+    if (!isLiveMode()) { this.skip(); } // Teams token tests require live AAD resource
     ...
  });
```
_(Exact file path to be confirmed by the package team; this is illustrative.)_

**Reproduced locally:** No — requires live AAD credentials and a provisioned Teams resource.

---

### `@azure/storage-blob`

**Failing tests (9, all new):**
- `BlobServiceClient > createContainer and deleteContainer`
- `BlockBlobClient > stageBlockFromURL copy source blob as single block`
- `BlockBlobClient > stageBlock, stageBlockURL and commitBlockList with CPK`
- `BlockBlobClient > stageBlockFromURL copy source blob as separate blocks`
- (5 additional related tests)

**Error:**
```
Public access is not permitted on this storage account.
RequestId: 7d29b1b7-b01e-00ab-10b4-b6320e000000
Time: 2026-03-18T08:55:16.0408214Z
````

**Root cause:**
The tests explicitly set **public (anonymous) access** on test containers:

- `blobserviceclient.spec.ts` calls `blobServiceClient.createContainer(containerName, { access: "container" })`.
- `blockblobclient.spec.ts` calls `containerClient.setAccessPolicy("container")`.

The CI storage account has recently been configured to **disallow public blob access** at the account level (a security hardening change, reflected in "9 new" failures with no prior occurrences). When the account-level policy blocks public access, these API calls are rejected with `PublicAccessNotPermitted`.

**Suggested fix:**
The tests that require public container access should be guarded with a `skipIfStorageAccountDoesNotSupportPublicAccess` helper, **or** refactored to not rely on public access (use SAS tokens instead). Minimally, add a conditional skip:

````diff
--- a/sdk/storage/storage-blob/test/blobserviceclient.spec.ts
+++ b/sdk/storage/storage-blob/test/blobserviceclient.spec.ts
@@ ... @@
   it("createContainer and deleteContainer", async function () {
+    if (!isLiveMode()) {
+      // Public-access containers are blocked on the CI storage account; skip in live mode.
+      this.skip();
+    }
     const blobServiceClient = getBSU(recorder);
```

Alternatively, the CI storage account should be re-evaluated: if public blob access must remain disabled for security reasons, the test suite must be updated to stop asserting `blobPublicAccess` or to use a separate storage account that allows it.

**Reproduced locally:** No — requires a live Azure Storage account with the same account-level public access policy.

---

### `@azure/communication-short-codes`

**Failing tests (7 total: 1 new, 6 recurring):**
- `ShortCodesClient - manage Attachments > can manage Attachments` _(1 new)_
- Additional recurring test cases (not individually annotated in CI output)

**Error:**
```
The server encountered an internal error.
```

**Root cause:**
The Communication Short Codes service returns HTTP 500 for live attachment-management operations in the CI environment. This is a server-side error from the Azure Communication Services backend, not from any change in the SDK. The majority of failures (6 of 7) are recurring, confirming this is a pre-existing live-service instability.

**Suggested fix:**
No SDK code change is actionable. The Azure Communication Services team should investigate the backend internal error. Test should be marked as live-only with appropriate retry logic, or skipped until the service-side issue is resolved.

**Reproduced locally:** No — requires live Communication Services credentials.

---

### `@azure-rest/ai-document-intelligence`

**Failing tests:** 0 (all 135 tests **pass**)

**Failing CI steps:**
- `js - documentintelligence (Build Build)` — script exited with code 1
- `js - documentintelligence (Build Analyze)` — `PowerShell exited with code '1'`

**Error:**
```
Script failed with exit code: 1
PowerShell exited with code: 1
````
_(No test-failure annotations; build/analyze step annotations only point to log line numbers in Azure Pipelines, which are not accessible from this environment.)_

**Root cause:**
The `build` and `extract-api` (API Extractor) pipeline steps are failing even though all unit tests pass. The most likely cause is that the generated `review/ai-document-intelligence-node.api.md` file is **out of sync** with the current source — i.e., a recent source change altered the public API surface and the review file was not regenerated. API Extractor would exit non-zero in this case.

To confirm, run locally:
````bash
pnpm turbo build --filter=`@azure-rest/ai-document-intelligence...` --token 1
cd sdk/documentintelligence/ai-document-intelligence-rest
pnpm run extract-api
git diff review/
```

**Suggested fix:**
If `git diff` shows changes after `extract-api`, commit the updated `review/*.api.md` file. If the API surface change was intentional, the diff should be reviewed and approved through the standard API review process.

**Reproduced locally:** Not yet attempted — blocked on full package build (turbo).

---

### `@azure/openai`

**Failing tests (311 total: 295 recurring, 16 new):**
- `AbortSignal [2024-10-21] > chat.completions.stream.events.abort > 'gpt-4o' ('2024-05-13')`
- `Legacy Completions [2025-04-01-preview] > completions.create > returns completions across all models > 'gpt-4o' ('2024-05-13')`
- `Legacy Completions [2025-04-01-preview] > completions.create > returns completions stream > 'gpt-4o' ('2024-05-13')`
- `Whisper > [2025-04-01-preview] Client > audio.transcriptions.create > returns json transcription for mp3 files`
- (307 additional tests)

**Error:**
```
429 Too Many Requests
Your requests to gpt-4o-ais-deployment-test for gpt-4o in East US have exceeded
the call rate limit for your current OpenAI S0 pricing tier.
Please retry after 3 seconds. To increase your default rate limit, visit:
(aka.ms/redacted)
BaseURL: (sdk8428.openai.azure.com/redacted)
Deployment: gpt-4o-ais-deployment-test
````

**Root cause:**
The CI Azure OpenAI deployment (`gpt-4o-ais-deployment-test`, East US, S0 tier) is being **rate-limited** (HTTP 429). With 295 recurring failures out of 311 total, this is a long-standing quota/throttling issue in the CI environment. The 16 new failures may reflect additional tests added or increased parallelism. The `Whisper` failure ("Test failed", without a 429 error) may be a separate, unrelated flake.

**Suggested fix:**
No SDK code change resolves rate-limiting. Options for the team:
1. Increase the quota on the `sdk8428.openai.azure.com` deployment via (aka.ms/redacted)
2. Switch to a higher-tier (S1 or PTU) deployment for CI.
3. Reduce test parallelism or add retry-with-backoff logic in the test harness.
4. Segregate rate-limited live tests into a separate, less-frequent CI stage.

**Reproduced locally:** No — requires live Azure OpenAI credentials and quota.

---

## Additional Notes

- **All failures are live-service or infrastructure issues.** No failures were traced to a logic regression in the TypeScript source code shipped in these packages.
- The `@azure/storage-blob` "public access not permitted" failures (9 **new**) stand out as a likely recent infrastructure change to the CI storage account. This is the only case where failures appeared for the first time in this CI run and may benefit from a storage account configuration review.
- The `@azure-rest/ai-document-intelligence` build-analyze failure is the only case that *could* require a source-file change (regenerating the API review file), but it cannot be confirmed without access to the Azure Pipelines build log.
- The `@azure/openai` recurring failures (295/311) represent a chronic CI quota problem that has been present for multiple CI runs.
- CI check runs were inspected on commit [`4adaf9f`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]) (HEAD of `main` as of 2026-03-18).
