Sync eng/common directory with azure-sdk-tools for PR","author":{"name":"Azure SDK Bot","email":"53356347+azure-sdk@users.noreply.github.com","date":"2026-04-16T18:24:14Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-16T18:24:14Z"}},"author":{"login":"azure-sdk","id":53356347,"profile_url":"https://github.com/azure-sdk","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/53356347?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"Add issue-triage gh-aw workflow (#38153)

## What
Port the intelligent issue triage workflow from
[azure-sdk-for-net](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/.github/workflows/issue-triage.md)
to this repository.

## Why
Automate initial issue triage to reduce manual effort — classify issues,
predict labels, look up owners, and route to the right team members.

## What the workflow does
1. **Retrieve & validate** — fetches new issue, skips if already labeled
2. **Customer evaluation** — classifies author as team member, customer,
or bot using `author_association`
3. **Label prediction** — predicts category (`Client`/`Mgmt`) and
service labels from issue content
4. **Deprecated package check** — queries npm registry; auto-closes
issues for fully deprecated packages
5. **Owner lookup** — scans `CODEOWNERS` `ServiceLabel` entries
bottom-to-top, assigns owners
6. **Routing comment** — pings owners via `mention_owners` step
7. **Analysis comment** — posts structured triage summary with label
confidence and routing details

## Adaptations from .NET
- npm registry instead of NuGet for deprecation checks
- JS category labels (`Client`, `Mgmt`, `Central-EngSys`, `Mgmt-EngSys`,
`Service`)
- Package naming (`@azure/storage-blob` format)
- CODEOWNERS examples updated for JS repo structure
- Created `bot` label for bot-filed issue classification
- Compiled with gh-aw v0.68.3

## Problem

`createFile` and `encodeByteArray` silently corrupt data when given a
`Uint8Array` subarray (via `.subarray()`). Both read from the full
backing `ArrayBuffer` instead of the subarray range.

## Fix

- **`createFile`** (`core-rest-pipeline`): copy subarrays that do not
span the full buffer
- **`encodeByteArray`** (`core-client`): pass `byteOffset`/`byteLength`
to `Buffer.from()`

Tests added for both.

shell-substituted repo root and `pnpm-lock.yaml`.

### `steps-after-generations.md` — missing `...` suffix on three `pnpm
build --filter` commands
The Linux record block already had `...` (so dependencies are built);
the other three (Windows record, Linux playback, Windows playback) were
inconsistent and have been fixed to match.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>","author":{"name":"Deyaa","email":"dealmaha@microsoft.com","date":"2026-04-14T20:42:22Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-14T20:42:22Z"}},"author":{"login":"deyaaeldeen","id":6074665,"profile_url":"https://github.com/deyaaeldeen","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6074665?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"chore(core): add react-native and @types/react devDependencies (#38135)

Add `react-native` and `@types/react` as devDependencies to all 14 core
packages. Required for type-checking the react-native build target.

## Additional fixes

**`tsconfig.browser.base.json`** — Exclude `src/**/*-react-native.*`
from browser test compilation. Without this, `tsc` picks up react-native
platform files (e.g. `runtimeInfo-react-native.mts`) which `import
"react-native"`, pulling in globals that conflict with DOM types.

**`core-amqp/src/util/utils.ts`** — Change `_timer` type from
`ReturnType<typeof setTimeout>` to `number | NodeJS.Timeout`. The root
`tsconfig.src.react-native.json` inherits `types: ["node"]` from
`tsconfig.src.build.json`, so both `@types/node` and react-native
globals are in scope. React-native overrides `setTimeout` to return
`number` while `@types/node` returns `NodeJS.Timeout`, and the local
`class Timeout` shadows the type name, causing a conflict. This is
resolved properly in the `#platform/*` migration where the react-native
tsconfig uses `types: ["react-native"]` only.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>","author":{"name":"Deyaa","email":"dealmaha@microsoft.com","date":"2026-04-14T20:41:43Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-14T20:41:43Z"}},"author":{"login":"deyaaeldeen","id":6074665,"profile_url":"https://github.com/deyaaeldeen","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6074665?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"fix(warp): rebase #imports paths in virtual CJS package.json (#38106)

When warp builds a CJS target, it places a virtual `package.json` inside
`src/` to set `"type": "commonjs"`. It copies the `imports` field from
the real `package.json` at the package root, but the paths in that field
(e.g. `"./src/base64.ts"`) are relative to the package root. Since
TypeScript resolves them relative to the virtual `package.json` in
`src/`, it looks for `src/src/base64.ts` — which does not exist. Files
resolved through `#imports` are silently dropped from the CJS output.

## Fix

`rebaseImportPaths()` rewrites the paths so they are correct relative to
the virtual `package.json` location. For example, `"./src/base64.ts"`
becomes `"./base64.ts"`.

Also handles upward-relative paths (`../`), array fallbacks, and rebases
the `exports` field for consistency (though `exports` does not affect
compilation in practice — only self-referencing imports would use it).

Removes redundant `customConditions` from `EMIT_AFFECTING_OPTIONS`
(already captured by `resolvedImports` since #38000).

## Testing

- Integration test verifying CJS target emits all files resolved through
`#imports`
- Unit tests for `rebaseImportPaths` covering nested conditions, arrays,
upward paths, bare specifiers, and no-op cases
- All 280 warp tests pass

Co-authored-by: Ray Chen <raychen@microsoft.com>","author":{"name":"Azure SDK Bot","email":"53356347+azure-sdk@users.noreply.github.com","date":"2026-04-14T00:48:58Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-14T00:48:58Z"}},"author":{"login":"azure-sdk","id":53356347,"profile_url":"https://github.com/azure-sdk","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/53356347?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"feat(ts-http-runtime): migrate to #platform/* subpath imports (#37974)

## Migrate `@typespec/ts-http-runtime` to `#platform/*` subpath imports

Replaces warp's `polyfillSuffix` mechanism with Node.js [subpath
imports](https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html#subpath-imports)
(`#platform/*`) for all platform-specific code in `ts-http-runtime`.

### Why

`polyfillSuffix` works by silently swapping files at build time (e.g.
`foo.ts` → `foo-browser.mts`) based on the warp target. This is
invisible to TypeScript — all platform variants share one set of type
declarations, so the compiler can't distinguish what's available on each
target. It also forces runtime environment sniffing for any logic that
needs to know its platform, which is fundamentally unreliable in hybrid
environments.

Node.js subpath imports solve both problems: each platform variant gets
its own types (caught at compile time), and the correct module is
selected by the package manager/bundler resolving `package.json`
conditions — no runtime guesswork.

Concrete benefits:

- **Type safety** — each platform variant is independently type-checked.
For example, the Node `isNodeReadableStream` returns `x is Readable`
(from `node:stream`), the browser version uses `instanceof
ReadableStream`, and RN returns `x is never`. The old approach silently
produced incorrect type narrowing on non-Node platforms.

- **Environment detection correctness** — the old `checkEnvironment.ts`
ran `typeof window`, `typeof process`, etc. on every platform, producing
wrong results in Electron, Cloudflare Workers, Deno, and React Native
(see [linked issues](#runtime-detection-issues) below). Per-platform
`env` modules use static constants that are compile-time truths.

- **Simpler testing** — the old test scripts required `dev-tool run
build-package` and `dev-tool run build-test` to manually resolve
polyfill suffixes before tests could run. With subpath imports, Vitest
resolves `#platform/*` modules natively via `resolve.conditions` — no
prebuild step needed:
  ```diff
-"test:browser": "npm run clean && dev-tool run build-package &&
dev-tool run build-test && dev-tool run test:vitest --no-test-proxy
--browser",
-"test:node": "dev-tool run build-test --no-browser-test && dev-tool run
test:vitest --no-test-proxy",
  +"test:browser": "dev-tool run test:vitest --no-test-proxy --browser",
  +"test:node": "dev-tool run test:vitest --no-test-proxy",
+"test:react-native": "dev-tool run test:vitest --no-test-proxy
--browser -- -c vitest.react-native.config.ts",
  ```

- **Less code** — where browser and RN share identical implementations
(no-ops, web APIs), a single `-web.mts` file replaces two duplicates,
following the pattern already established by `sha256-web.mts`,
`inspect-web.mts`, etc.

### What changed

**Platform modules** — platform-specific code extracted into modules
resolved via `#platform/*` entries in `package.json#imports`:

| Import | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `#platform/encoding` | UTF-8 encode/decode |
| `#platform/env` | environment constants (`isBrowser`, `isNodeLike`,
etc.) |
| `#platform/formData` | form data preparation |
| `#platform/policies` | platform-specific pipeline policies |
| `#platform/types` | platform type re-exports (`FormData`, `Blob`,
`File`, etc.) |
| `#platform/typeGuards` | `isNodeReadableStream` /
`isWebReadableStream` |

Each has a Node (`.ts`), browser (`.mts`), and react-native (`.mts`)
variant selected at build time by warp target conditions and at test
time by Vite's `resolve.conditions`.

**Config** — package-level tsconfigs moved to `config/` subdirectory,
extending shared configs from `eng/tsconfigs/`. ESLint updated to use
`config/tsconfig.lint.json` with `projectService: false`.

**Deleted** — `checkEnvironment.ts`, redundant browser/RN duplicate
files, old root-level tsconfigs (`tsconfig.src.json`,
`tsconfig.test.json`, `tsconfig.test.node.json`,
`tsconfig.browser.config.json`, `tsconfig.samples.json`,
`tsconfig.snippets.json`).

### Runtime detection issues

The migration structurally eliminates a class of recurring bugs caused
by runtime environment sniffing:

**Electron** — both `isBrowser` and `isNodeLike` true; bundler served
browser files that threw on Node-only policies:
- #12983, #14025, #21110, #30123

**Cloudflare / Deno / edge** — `nodejs_compat` flags set
`process.versions.node`, triggering Node-only policies the runtime
doesn't support:
- #27077, #28672, #37345 (open — user workaround patches
`process.versions.node = undefined`)

**React Native** — `process` shims made `isNodeLike=true`, selecting
Node code paths that crash:
- #20787, #26609, #30411

### Packages impacted by this PR

`@azure/notification-hubs`

### Issues associated with this PR

`@azure/notification-hubs` does not re-export `RestError` or
`isRestError` from `@azure/core-rest-pipeline`, unlike other Azure SDKs
(`@azure/cosmos`, `@azure/storage-queue`, `@azure/data-tables`, etc.).
This forces users to add `@azure/core-rest-pipeline` as a direct
dependency for basic error handling, and IDEs may auto-import
`RestError` from unrelated packages.

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

Adds re-exports of `RestError` and `isRestError` from
`@azure/core-rest-pipeline` to the package's public API surface,
consistent with other Azure SDK packages. The package version is bumped
from `2.0.3` to `2.1.0` (minor semver bump for new public API exports)
with a corresponding changelog entry. The `SDK_VERSION` constant in
`src/utils/constants.ts` is also updated to `2.1.0`.

```ts
import { RestError, isRestError } from "@azure/notification-hubs";

try {
  // ...
} catch (e) {
  if (isRestError(e)) {
    console.log(e.statusCode);
  }
}
```

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If
there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR
chosen?

Direct re-export from the existing `@azure/core-rest-pipeline`
dependency — same pattern used by `@azure/cosmos`, `@azure/data-tables`,
and the storage packages.

### Are there test cases added in this PR? _(If not, why?)_

No. This is a pass-through re-export of symbols already tested in
`@azure/core-rest-pipeline`. The API review file is updated via
`extract-api`.

Co-authored-by: mikeharder <9459391+mikeharder@users.noreply.github.com>","author":{"name":"Azure SDK Bot","email":"53356347+azure-sdk@users.noreply.github.com","date":"2026-04-13T17:59:57Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-13T17:59:57Z"}},"author":{"login":"azure-sdk","id":53356347,"profile_url":"https://github.com/azure-sdk","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/53356347?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"Enable fork PR support and fix race condition in review workflows (#38105)

## What

Switch all 7 review workflows (archie, dash, dexter, mgmt-review,
scribe, sentinel, tester) from `pull_request` to `pull_request_target`
to enable reviews on fork PRs. This follows the pattern established in
[azure-rest-api-specs#42268](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/42268).

## Changes

- **`pull_request` → `pull_request_target`** with `forks: ["*"]` —
allows workflows to run on fork PRs
- **`workflow_dispatch`** with `item_number` input — enables manual
re-runs
- **Label removal step** in `on.steps` (pre-activation) — removes the
trigger label since `label_command` isn't used with
`pull_request_target`
- **Removed `fork == false` guard** from `if:` conditions — no longer
needed since the label gate (only collaborators can apply labels)
provides the security boundary
- **Label-aware concurrency group** — includes `github.event.label.name`
in the concurrency key to prevent a race condition where applying
multiple review labels simultaneously causes one workflow run to cancel
another (see #38093)

## Security

`pull_request_target` runs from the base branch context with write token
access. This is safe because:
1. Only collaborators can apply trigger labels (label gate)
2. The workflows read PR content via API calls, not by checking out fork
code
3. No untrusted code from the fork is executed

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>","author":{"name":"Azure SDK Bot","email":"53356347+azure-sdk@users.noreply.github.com","date":"2026-04-10T20:44:08Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-10T20:44:08Z"}},"author":{"login":"azure-sdk","id":53356347,"profile_url":"https://github.com/azure-sdk","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/53356347?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"[event-hubs] Reduce JavaScript Event Hubs send and receive hot-path overhead (#37882)

## Summary

This PR improves several internal JavaScript Event Hubs send and receive
hot paths.

Included changes:

- fix double encoding in idempotent batches
- cache repeated partition key hashing results
- wake receive waits from queue notifications before polling fallback
- add fast paths for string and primitive payload encoding
- skip JSON parsing for bodies that do not look like JSON
- avoid cloning `EventData` when no tracing properties are present

## What Changed

- Reduce duplicate encoding work in idempotent producer paths.
- Cache repeated partition-key hash inputs instead of reallocating
conversion buffers for the same keys.
- Make receive-side waiting event-driven before falling back to polling.
- Add cheaper send-side payload transformation paths for strings and
simple primitives.
- Avoid unconditional JSON parsing for received bodies that do not look
like JSON.
- Skip tracing-related object cloning when no instrumentation properties
need to be added.

## Validation

- Full package Node test suite passed with `npm run test:node` in
`sdk/eventhub/event-hubs`.
- The checked-in benchmark harnesses were used as directional validation
for both send-side and receive-side changes.
- `js-benchmark/benchmark.mjs` now reports receive subscription
initialization, warm-up, send-call time, and matched-event callback
latency separately, uses bounded client retry timeouts, and inserts
short cool-down periods between send-heavy sections to avoid emulator
throttling windows.
- Recent local emulator runs showed stable matched-event callback
latency around `30-31 ms` for both the receive-latency and
receive-decode cases once those throttle windows were separated from the
measured path.

## Risk

Risk is low to moderate and localized to internal send/receive hot
paths.

- Most changes are allocation and fast-path optimizations without public
API changes.
- The receive queue wake-up change is the most behavior-sensitive item,
but it is covered by the package test suite.","author":{"name":"Alvaro Videla","email":"videlalvaro@gmail.com","date":"2026-04-10T19:31:09Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-10T19:31:09Z"}},"author":{"login":"videlalvaro","id":30834,"profile_url":"https://github.com/videlalvaro","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/30834?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"Fix race condition in label-triggered review workflows (#38093)

### Problem

When multiple review labels (e.g. `docs-review-needed` +
`test-review-needed`) are applied to a PR simultaneously, review
workflows silently fail to run.

**Root cause**: GitHub Actions `pull_request: types: [labeled]` fires
for *any* label — there is no way to filter by specific label at the
trigger level. So each label event triggers **all 7** review workflows.
The `if:` condition on each job correctly skips non-matching labels, but
the concurrency group (`gh-aw-<workflow>-<PR#>`) is evaluated first.

When the same workflow is triggered twice in quick succession (once per
label event), `cancel-in-progress: true` cancels the first run — which
may be the one whose label actually matched. The second run then skips
because its label does not match. Net result: the workflow never
completes.

**Example**: On
[#37993](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/pull/37993) at
`2026-04-10T15:32:11Z`, Documentation Review was cancelled by a
`test-review-needed` label event and never ran.

### Fix

Include `github.event.label.name` in the concurrency group for all 7
label-triggered review workflows.

- **Same workflow + same label** → same group → `cancel-in-progress`
works as intended
- **Same workflow + different label** → different groups → no
interference

### Why not make the trigger more specific?

GitHub Actions does not support filtering `labeled` events by label name
at the trigger level. Filtering can only happen via job-level `if:`
conditions, which execute after the concurrency group is resolved. The
concurrency group fix is the only viable approach.

### Affected workflows

`archie`, `dash`, `dexter`, `mgmt-review`, `scribe`, `sentinel`,
`tester`

## Problem

Agentic workflow review agents (Architecture Review, Security Review,
etc.) were silently failing because Copilot CLI v1.0.22 blocked the
`github` and `safeoutputs` MCP servers at container startup:

```
! 2 MCP servers were blocked by policy: 'github', 'safeoutputs'
```

This caused agents to:
- Fall back to raw `git diff` commands instead of using GitHub API tools
(wasting tokens)
- Be unable to post review comments via
`create_pull_request_review_comment` (tool does not exist)
- Report **success** despite producing zero output (`agent_output.json:
{"items":[]}`)

**Example**: [Architecture Review run
24220062323](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/actions/runs/24220062323)
on PR #37993 consumed 2.94M tokens across 66 turns but posted zero
review comments.

## Root Cause

Tracked in github/gh-aw#25550 — fixed in gh-aw v0.67.4+.

## Changes

- `github/gh-aw-actions/setup`: v0.67.3 → v0.68.0
- `github/gh-aw/actions/setup`: v0.67.3 → v0.68.0
- `actions/github-script`: v8 → v9.0.0 (SHA-pinned)
- `mgmt-review.lock.yml`: strict mode enabled (compiler default)
- All 9 workflows recompiled with latest compiler

## Verification

After merge, re-trigger any review agent on a PR with the appropriate
label and confirm:
- No `MCP servers were blocked by policy` message in agent logs
- Review comments are successfully posted to the PR

### Packages impacted by this PR

`.scripts/automation_generate.sh`

### Issues associated with this PR

Bump emitter version

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

TypeSpec emitter dependencies in `eng/emitter-package.json` were pinned
to stale versions. Updated all packages to latest:

| Package | Old | New |
|---------|-----|-----|
| `@azure-tools/typespec-ts` | `0.52.0` | `0.52.1` |
| `@azure-tools/typespec-azure-core` | `0.66.1` | `0.67.0` |
| `@azure-tools/typespec-autorest` | `0.66.1` | `0.67.0` |
| `@azure-tools/typespec-client-generator-core` | `0.66.4` | `0.67.1` |
| `@azure-tools/typespec-azure-resource-manager` | `0.66.1` | `0.67.0` |
| `@azure-tools/typespec-azure-rulesets` | `0.66.0` | `0.67.0` |
| `@azure-tools/openai-typespec` | `1.13.1` | `1.14.0` |
| `@typespec/compiler` | `1.10.0` | `1.11.0` |
| `@typespec/http` | `1.10.0` | `1.11.0` |
| `@typespec/openapi` | `1.10.0` | `1.11.0` |
| `@typespec/openapi3` | `1.10.0` | `1.11.0` |
| `@typespec/rest` | `0.80.0` | `0.81.0` |
| `@typespec/versioning` | `0.80.0` | `0.81.0` |
| `@typespec/events` | `0.80.0` | `0.81.0` |
| `@typespec/streams` | `0.80.0` | `0.81.0` |

Also bumped `@autorest/typescript` range in
`.scripts/automation_generate.sh`: `^6.0.66` → `^6.0.67`.

Lock file regenerated via `tsp-client generate-lock-file`.

Syncs `eng/tools/js-sdk-release-tools` with azure-sdk-tools [PR
#15030](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-tools/pull/15030), which
released `@azure-tools/js-sdk-release-tools@2.16.7`.

### Packages impacted by this PR
`eng/tools/js-sdk-release-tools`

### Issues associated with this PR


### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR
`eng/tools/js-sdk-release-tools/package.json` was pinned to
`@azure-tools/js-sdk-release-tools@2.16.6`. This PR tracks the 2.16.7
release published from azure-sdk-tools.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If
there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR
chosen?
N/A — straightforward version pin update.

### Are there test cases added in this PR? _(If not, why?)_
No — version bump only; no logic changes.

- azure-sdk-tools [PR
#15030](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-tools/pull/15030)

## What

Adds shared vitest configs under `common/vitestconfigs/` and excludes
react-native test directories from default test runs.

## Why

### Platform-specific testing without building

Vitest uses Vite's module resolution under the hood, which supports the
`resolve.conditions` option. By setting conditions like `["browser"]` or
`["react-native"]`, Vite resolves `#platform/*` subpath imports (from
`package.json` `imports`) to the correct platform variant **at transform
time** — no separate `tsc` build step needed.

This means:
- **No per-platform builds for testing**: Unlike `tsc`, which requires
separate tsconfig files and build passes for each platform, Vite handles
the resolution and transpilation in a single step.
- **Tests run directly against source**: Vite transforms TypeScript
source on the fly with the correct platform conditions, so tests import
the right platform code without needing `dist/` output.
- **Simpler CI**: One vitest invocation per platform (node, browser,
react-native) replaces the build-then-test dance.

### Root excludes

The root vitest excludes prevent react-native tests from accidentally
running during default node/browser test runs, which would fail since
they require RN-specific resolve conditions.

## Changes

### New shared configs (`common/vitestconfigs/`)
- `browser.config.ts` — browser vitest config with `conditions:
["browser"]`, excludes node and react-native test directories
- `react-native.config.ts` — RN vitest config with `conditions:
["react-native"]`, a `stubReactNative()` Vite plugin to shim the
`react-native` import, and excludes node and browser test directories

### Root vitest excludes
- `vitest.shared.config.ts` — added `test/**/react-native/**` to
excludes
- `vitest.browser.base.config.ts` — added `dist-test/**/react-native/**`
to excludes

## Usage

A package re-exports the shared config in its own vitest config:
```ts
export { default } from "../../../common/vitestconfigs/browser.config.ts";
```

No packages consume these configs yet — this PR just adds the
infrastructure.

## What

Adds shared TypeScript config files under `common/tsconfigs/` to support
packages that organize their tsconfigs into a `config/` subdirectory.

## Why

Reduce duplication across packages that need per-platform build/test
configs (ESM, CJS, browser, React Native). The shared configs use
TypeScript's `${configDir}/..` pattern so that leaf configs placed in a
package's `config/` subdirectory resolve paths relative to the package
root.

## Changes

### New shared configs (`common/tsconfigs/`)
- `base.src.json` / `base.test.json` — common base configs for src
builds and tests
- `src.esm.json`, `src.cjs.json`, `src.browser.json`,
`src.react-native.json` — platform-specific src builds
- `test.node.json`, `test.browser.json`, `test.react-native.json` —
platform-specific test configs
- `snippets.json` — shared snippets config

### Root tsconfig.json
- Changed `lib` from `[]` to `["ES2023"]` so shared configs can inherit
it instead of redeclaring

### Dev-tool
- `extract-api.ts`: `getTsconfigFile()` now checks the `config/`
subdirectory when locating a package's tsconfig for API extraction

## Note on root tsconfig files

The other `tsconfig.*.json` files in the repo root (e.g.
`tsconfig.browser.base.json`, `tsconfig.src.esm.json`,
`tsconfig.test.base.json`, etc.) are **legacy** and will be deleted once
all packages migrate to the new shared configs under
`common/tsconfigs/`.

## Usage

A package's leaf config in `config/tsconfig.src.esm.json`:
```json
{
  "extends": "../../../../common/tsconfigs/src.esm.json",
  "include": ["../src/**/*.ts", "../src/**/*.mts"]
}
```

No packages consume these configs yet — this PR just adds the
infrastructure.

## What

Add `react-native` as a devDependency to `@typespec/ts-http-runtime`,
replacing the hand-written type shim with real package types, and give
`concat-react-native.mts` its own polyfill-friendly implementation.

## Why

### Hermes implements a subset of web APIs — its types diverge from DOM

React Native uses the [Hermes](https://hermesengine.dev/) JavaScript
engine, which provides its own implementations of web APIs (`Blob`,
`FormData`, `TextDecoder`, etc.) rather than deferring to a full browser
engine. Hermes does **not** implement the complete DOM/Web API surface —
its built-in types are a **subset** of what `lib.dom.d.ts` declares:

- **`Blob` constructor** — accepts only `(Blob | string)[]`, not the
full `BlobPart[]` union (`ArrayBufferView` / `ArrayBuffer` / `string`).
Passing a `Uint8Array` throws at runtime. See
[facebook/react-native#44125](https://github.com/facebook/react-native/issues/44125).
Thanks @jeremymeng for the investigation.
- **`Uint8Array` → `Blob`** — works as a data type, but cannot be passed
to the Blob constructor without a polyfill. See
[facebook/react-native#41079](https://github.com/facebook/react-native/issues/41079).
Thanks @jeremymeng for the investigation.
- **`ReadableStream` / `Response`** — not available by default. Apps can
polyfill these.
- **`Platform` API** — React Native-specific, no DOM equivalent.
- **`globals.d.ts`** — react-native ships its own global type
declarations that overlap with but are narrower than `lib.dom.d.ts`,
causing type conflicts when both are loaded.

This means our hand-written `react-native.d.ts` shim (which only
declared `Platform`) was incomplete, and we were missing type errors
that surface only under the real react-native types.

## Changes

- **`pnpm-workspace.yaml`** — added `react-native: ^0.84.1` and
`@types/react: ^19.2.14` to the `testing` catalog
- **`sdk/core/ts-http-runtime/package.json`** — added `react-native:
catalog:testing` and `@types/react: catalog:testing` to devDependencies
- **`src/util/react-native.d.ts`** — deleted (replaced by real package
types)
- **`src/util/concat-react-native.mts`** — standalone RN implementation
that handles all source types (`Blob`, `Uint8Array`, `ReadableStream`)
and defers to the runtime. Apps with appropriate polyfills get correct
behavior; apps without them get the engine's native error. Uses a type
assertion (`as (Blob | string)[]`) to satisfy RN's stricter `BlobPart`
types at compile time. Includes detailed JSDoc documenting Hermes
limitations with links to upstream issues. Thanks @timovv for the great
discussion.
- **`src/util/concat.common.ts` → `src/util/concat-browser.mts`** —
since `concat-react-native.mts` is now standalone, `concat.common.ts`
was only used by the browser target. Inlined its content directly into
`concat-browser.mts` and deleted the now-unused common file.
- **`tsconfig.browser.config.json`** — added `skipLibCheck: true` to
avoid type conflicts between react-native's `globals.d.ts` (Hermes
subset types) and `lib.dom.d.ts` (full browser types)
- **`pnpm-lock.yaml`** — updated with react-native and transitive
dependencies

PR #38054 compiled lock files against stale `.md` files that didn't
include the trigger changes from #38035 (`pull_request_target` →
`pull_request`). This caused the frontmatter hash stored in the lock
files to mismatch what the runtime checker computes, failing all agentic
workflows with:

```
ERR_CONFIG: Lock file is outdated! The workflow file frontmatter has changed.
```

This PR recompiles all lock files from the current `.md` files so the
hashes match.

### Packages impacted

None — workflow/CI changes only.

## Security: Replace `pull_request_target` with `pull_request` in
agentic workflows

Switches all 7 agentic review workflows (archie, dash, dexter,
mgmt-review, scribe, sentinel, tester) from `pull_request_target` to
`pull_request` to reduce their privilege surface.

### Why this matters

`pull_request_target` runs in the **base branch context** with full
access to repository secrets and a write-enabled `GITHUB_TOKEN` — even
for PRs from forks. This is an unnecessary privilege escalation for
workflows that only perform read-only AI-powered code reviews.

Using `pull_request` instead follows the [principle of least
privilege](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-for-github-actions/security-guides/security-hardening-for-github-actions#understanding-the-risk-of-script-injections):
no secrets are exposed for fork PRs and the token is read-only.

### What changed

1. **Trigger**: `pull_request_target` → `pull_request` across all 7
workflow `.md` and `.lock.yml` files.
2. **Fork guard**: Added `github.event.pull_request.head.repo.fork ==
false` to job conditions so workflows skip gracefully on forked PRs
(where secrets are unavailable) instead of failing.
3. **Removed workaround**: Deleted the `pull_request_target` checkout
instructions from `mgmt-review.md` that were only needed because the old
trigger checked out the base branch instead of the PR branch.

### References

- [GitHub Security Lab — Preventing pwn
requests](https://securitylab.github.com/resources/github-actions-preventing-pwn-requests/)
- [GitHub Docs — Security hardening for GitHub
Actions](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-for-github-actions/security-guides/security-hardening-for-github-actions)
- [GitHub Blog — `pull_request_target` and branch protection
changes](https://github.blog/changelog/2025-11-07-actions-pull_request_target-and-environment-branch-protections-changes/)

### Packages impacted

None — workflow/CI changes only.

Co-authored-by: deyaaeldeen <6074665+deyaaeldeen@users.noreply.github.com>","author":{"name":"Copilot","email":"198982749+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com","date":"2026-04-08T17:09:15Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-08T17:09:15Z"}},"author":{"login":"Copilot","id":198982749,"profile_url":"https://github.com/apps/copilot-swe-agent","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/in/1143301?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"refactor(warp): generalize esbuild transform to support any module format (#37999)

## What

Generalize warp's esbuild fast path from CJS-only to any module format,
so the same dedup logic works for both CJS and ESM derived targets.

## Changes

3 files changed in `common/tools/warp/`:

### compiler.ts
- **`buildTransformedOutput`** (renamed from `buildCjsFromSources`): Add
`format` parameter (`cjs` | `esm`). Per-file format selection ensures
`.mjs` files stay ESM and `.cjs` files stay CJS regardless of the
target's format — only `.js` files use the target format.
- **Source file discovery**: Derive source file list from the source
target's emitted output directory instead of `rootNames`, fixing issues
with entry-point includes where TypeScript emits files for the full
import graph.
- **`compileAllTargets`**: Generalize tracking from `esmTargetsByBaseId`
→ `emittedBaseTargets`. The first emitted target per base identity
becomes the source for any subsequent target with a different
`moduleType`. Transform path gating uses `emitId !== baseEmitId` instead
of checking `moduleType` truthiness, so ESM targets (which omit
`moduleType`) can also use the fast path.

### index.ts
- Update export name.

### test/public/build.spec.ts
- New test: verifies `.mts` → `.mjs` output preserves ESM format (uses
`export`, not `module.exports`) even in a CJS derived target.

## Why

Prerequisite for `#platform/*` subpath imports where browser/RN targets
may need ESM-to-ESM transforms with different condition resolution. See
#37974.

### Packages impacted by this PR

None (skill/agent documentation only)

### Issues associated with this PR

N/A

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

The `sdk-workflow` skill lacked any mention of the Azure DevOps npm feed
authentication step (`npx artifacts-npm-credprovider`). Agents following
the skill would hit `pnpm install` failures when adding new packages or
when auth is required, without knowing why or how to fix it. pnpm is
configured to install from the internal Azure Artifacts feed (devfeed);
anonymous read access works for packages already in the feed, but
authenticated access is required when installing new or updated
packages.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If
there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR
chosen?

Added a dedicated bullet under **Important Notes** in
`.github/skills/sdk-workflow/SKILL.md` that:
- States the command (`npx artifacts-npm-credprovider` at repo root)
- Explains *when* to run it: when `pnpm install` fails due to feed
authentication errors, or when adding new packages not yet cached for
anonymous access
- Explains *why* it's needed (pnpm uses the internal Azure Artifacts
devfeed; anonymous read works for existing packages but auth is needed
for new/updated ones)
- Points to `CONTRIBUTING.md` § "Authenticating to the Azure DevOps npm
feed" for full details

Placing it in **Important Notes** (rather than the table) ensures it's
not missed when troubleshooting `pnpm install` failures.

### Are there test cases added in this PR? _(If not, why?)_

No — documentation-only change.

### Provide a list of related PRs _(if any)_

N/A

create an Issue in the
[Autorest/typescript](https://github.com/Azure/autorest.typescript)

Sync .github directory with azure-sdk-tools for PR

Co-authored-by: jeremymeng <7583839+jeremymeng@users.noreply.github.com>","author":{"name":"Copilot","email":"198982749+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com","date":"2026-04-07T19:20:12Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-07T19:20:12Z"}},"author":{"login":"Copilot","id":198982749,"profile_url":"https://github.com/apps/copilot-swe-agent","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/in/1143301?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"Add missing polyfillSuffix entries to warp.config.yml for .cts polyfill packages (#38028)

Two packages with CommonJS dialect polyfill files (`.cts`) were missing
the `polyfillSuffix: "-cjs"` declaration on their `commonjs` warp
target, which tells the warp build tool to pick up CJS-specific polyfill
implementations.

### Packages impacted by this PR

- `@azure/api-management-custom-widgets-scaffolder`
- `@azure/monitor-opentelemetry`

### Issues associated with this PR

N/A

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

Scanned all packages for `.mts`/`.cts` polyfill files under `src/` and
cross-referenced with `warp.config.yml` target entries. Two packages had
`.cts` polyfills but no `polyfillSuffix: "-cjs"` on their `commonjs`
target:

| Package | Polyfill file |
|---|---|
| `api-management-custom-widgets-scaffolder` | `src/sourceDir-cjs.cts`
(exports `__dirname`) |
| `monitor-opentelemetry` | `src/shared/module-cjs.cts`
(`__dirname`/`__filename` + module URL helpers) |

Without `polyfillSuffix`, the build does not substitute the CJS-specific
implementation when producing the CommonJS bundle.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If
there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR
chosen?

Single approach: add `polyfillSuffix: "-cjs"` to the `commonjs` target
in each affected `warp.config.yml`, matching the pattern already
established across other packages (e.g., `core-client`, `core-tracing`).
Neither package uses `#platform/*` subpath imports, so there is no
conflict risk.

### Are there test cases added in this PR? _(If not, why?)_

No. This is a build configuration change; correctness is verified by the
warp build tool consuming the updated config.

N/A

Co-authored-by: deyaaeldeen <6074665+deyaaeldeen@users.noreply.github.com>","author":{"name":"Copilot","email":"198982749+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com","date":"2026-04-07T18:04:05Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-07T18:04:05Z"}},"author":{"login":"Copilot","id":198982749,"profile_url":"https://github.com/apps/copilot-swe-agent","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/in/1143301?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"refactor(ts-http-runtime): reorganize tests and fix imports (#38007)

## What

Reorganize `@typespec/ts-http-runtime` tests to correctly follow the
`test/public/` vs `test/internal/` convention, fix imports to use entry
points instead of direct source paths, and split platform-dependent
tests into browser/node variants.

## Convention

- **`test/public/`** — tests that import only from package entry points
(`.`, `./internal/util`, `./internal/policies`, `./internal/logger`)
- **`test/internal/`** — tests that import from non-exported source
paths (e.g., `src/client/clientHelpers.js`, `src/nodeHttpClient.js`)

## Changes

### 1. Test classification moves (public ↔ internal)

**public → internal** (3 files — these import non-exported source
paths):
- `pipeline.spec.ts` (imports `createPipelineFromOptions`)
- `credential.spec.ts` (also renamed directory `auth/` → `authPolicy/`)
- `client/getClient.spec.ts` (imports `clientHelpers`)

**internal → public** (14 files — these only use public entry points):
- `agentPolicy`, `defaultRetryPolicy`, `exponentialRetryPolicy`,
`formDataPolicy`, `multipartPolicy`, `redirectPolicy`, `retryPolicy`,
`systemErrorRetryPolicy`, `throttlingRetryPolicy`
- `browser/checkEnvironment`, `node/checkEnvironment`
- `logger/debug`
- `util/delay`, `util/uuidUtils`

### 2. Platform test splits

- **`formDataPolicy.spec.ts`** → shared (cross-platform) +
`browser/formDataPolicy.spec.ts` + `node/formDataPolicy.spec.ts`
- **`multipartPolicy.spec.ts`** → shared (cross-platform) +
`browser/multipartPolicy.spec.ts` + `node/multipartPolicy.spec.ts`
- **`client/multipart.spec.ts`** → Blob/File-dependent `buildBodyPart`
tests extracted to `browser/multipart.spec.ts` (removing `skipIf`
guards)
- **`assertBodyMatches` helper** removed from shared `test/util.ts` and
replaced with platform-specific `multipartTestHelper.ts` in `browser/`
and `node/`

### 3. Import rewrites

Replace direct source-path imports with barrel re-exports to prevent
cross-platform type leakage:

| Before (direct source path) | After (entry point / barrel) |
|---|---|
| `src/policies/retryPolicy.js` | `src/policies/internal.js` |
| `src/policies/formDataPolicy.js` | `src/policies/internal.js` |
| `src/policies/multipartPolicy.js` | `src/policies/internal.js` |
| `src/policies/redirectPolicy.js` | `src/policies/internal.js` |
| `src/policies/proxyPolicy.js` | `src/policies/internal.js` |
| `src/policies/decompressResponsePolicy.js` |
`src/policies/internal.js` |
| `src/policies/defaultRetryPolicy.js` | `src/policies/internal.js` |
| `src/policies/exponentialRetryPolicy.js` | `src/policies/internal.js`
|
| `src/policies/agentPolicy.js` | `src/policies/internal.js` |
| `src/util/bytesEncoding.js` | `src/util/internal.js` or `src/index.js`
|
| `src/util/checkEnvironment.js` | `src/util/internal.js` |
| `src/interfaces.js`, `src/logger/logger.js` | `src/index.js` |

### 4. File naming / location fixes

- `decompressResponsePolicy.ts` → `decompressResponsePolicy.spec.ts`
(browser + node — missing `.spec` suffix)
- `internal/util/sha256.spec.ts` → `internal/node/sha256.spec.ts`
(Node-specific crypto, belongs in `node/`)

### 5. New tests

- **Node platform policy tests** (`internal/node/pipeline.spec.ts`):
verify `createPipelineFromOptions` includes expected node-only policies,
correct ordering, and optional policy omission
- **Browser `buildBodyPart` tests**
(`internal/browser/multipart.spec.ts`): Blob content-type resolution,
File filename handling — extracted from shared file (were behind
`skipIf` guards)

### 6. Test logic fix

- `defaultLogPolicy.spec.ts`: replaced `isBrowser` with `isNodeLike` for
platform detection (correctly handles react-native, which is neither
browser nor Node)

## Verification
- `tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.test.node.json` ✅
- All tests pass ✅

{"total_count":2,"incomplete_results":false,"items":[{"id":4302052084,"number":38231,"state":"closed","state_reason":"completed","locked":false,"title":"CI test failures on main: `@azure/event-hubs`","body":"## Summary

CI analysis of commit [`50208323`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]) (2026-04-20) found **1 package** with actual unit test failures on the CI (playback) pipeline: **`@azure/event-hubs`**, with **9 failing tests** (all recurring). All other failing check runs were caused by infrastructure issues (OIDC token timeouts, missing artifact paths, browser runner exits, broken external links) with **0 test failures** in their test results.

The known failures tracking issue #37864 lists no known pre-existing failures, so these recurring failures are undocumented.

## Failures

### `@azure/event-hubs`

**Check run:** `js - eventhub (Build UnitTest ubuntu_22x_node)`  
**Test results:** 9 failed / 1,288 passed / 1,387 total (0 new, 9 recurring)

**Failing tests** in `sdk/eventhub/event-hubs/test/internal/client.spec.ts`:
- `EventHubClient > EventHubConsumerClient with non existent namespace > should throw ServiceCommunicationError for getEventHubProperties`
- `EventHubClient > EventHubConsumerClient with non existent namespace > should throw ServiceCommunicationError for getPartitionProperties`
- `EventHubClient > EventHubConsumerClient with non existent namespace > should throw ServiceCommunicationError for getPartitionIds`
- `EventHubClient > EventHubConsumerClient with non existent namespace > should throw ServiceCommunicationError while subscribe()`
- (and 5 more from the `EventHubProducerClient with non existent namespace` suite)

**Error (from CI annotations):**
```
expected AggregateError { errors: [ ...(4) ] } to be an instance of MessagingError
```

**Root cause:**

The tests use a fake connection string (`Endpoint=sb://a;...`) to verify that connecting to a non-existent namespace throws a `MessagingError`. The test helper `validateConnectionError` in the test file asserts:

```typescript
expect(err)
  .to.be.an.instanceOf(MessagingError)
  .and.has.property("code", isNodeLike ? "ENOTFOUND" : "ServiceCommunicationError");
```

In **Node.js 22+**, DNS resolution failures are reported as an `AggregateError` (aggregating IPv4 + IPv6 lookup failures) rather than a plain system error with `code: "ENOTFOUND"`. The `translate()` function in `sdk/core/core-amqp/src/errors.ts` does **not** handle `AggregateError` — it falls through to the last branch (`return isError(errObj) ? errObj : ...`) and returns the raw `AggregateError` untranslated. Because the raw `AggregateError` has no `.retryable` property, the retry loop breaks after one attempt and throws the single-element `errors[0]` = the raw `AggregateError` directly, bypassing the expected `MessagingError` wrapping.

**Suggested fix:**

Add `AggregateError` handling to `translate()` in `sdk/core/core-amqp/src/errors.ts`, immediately after the `TypeError`/`RangeError` guard:

```diff
--- a/sdk/core/core-amqp/src/errors.ts
+++ b/sdk/core/core-amqp/src/errors.ts
@@ -682,6 +682,12 @@ export function translate(err: unknown): MessagingError | Error {
   if (errObj instanceof TypeError || errObj instanceof RangeError) {
     return errObj;
   }
 
+  // Node.js 22+ reports DNS resolution failures as an AggregateError (multiple queries fail).
+  // Translate by recursively processing the first inner error, which carries the ENOTFOUND details.
+  if (errObj instanceof AggregateError && errObj.errors.length > 0) {
+    return translate(errObj.errors[0]);
+  }
+
   if (isAmqpError(errObj)) {
```

With this fix, when Node.js throws `AggregateError { errors: [{ code: "ENOTFOUND", syscall: "getaddrinfo", ... }] }`, `translate()` recurses on the first inner error, which is a system error recognized by `isSystemError()`. The result is a `MessagingError` with `code: "ENOTFOUND"` and `retryable: false`, exactly what the tests expect.

**Reproduced locally:** No — `pnpm install` could not complete in this environment because the workspace `.npmrc` points to a private Azure DevOps registry that requires authentication. The root cause was determined by static code analysis.

---

## Additional Notes

### Infrastructure-only failures (no test failures)

The following CI check runs also failed on the same commit but had **0 test failures** — their failures were purely infrastructural:

| Pattern | Affected packages | Infra error |
|---|---|---|
| `Build Build` — manifest generation | `batch`, `batch-mgmt`, `cognitiveservices-mgmt`, `links-mgmt`, `resources-mgmt` | `Path does not exist: /mnt/vss/_work/_temp/packages-dev-publish` |
| `Build UnitTest *_browser` — browser runner exit | `relationships-mgmt`, `tenantactivitylogalerts-mgmt`, `servicegroups-mgmt`, `previewalertrule-mgmt` | `Cmd.exe exited with code '255'` |
| `Build UnitTest macoslatest*` — test-proxy | `oracledatabase-mgmt`, `contentunderstanding`, `onlineexperimentation` | `Path does not exist: test-proxy.log` / OIDC token timeout |
| `Integration PublishDocsToNightlyBranch` | `containerregistry`, `storage`, `textanalytics`, `tables` | `PowerShell exited with code '1'` |
| `Aggregate Reports GenerateReports` | aggregate-reports | Broken external link `(tsx.is/redacted) + PoliCheck errors |

These are not code regressions and are not included in this issue.

failures on main: broken pnpm-lock.yaml causes widespread pnpm install failures","body":"## Summary

A broken `pnpm-lock.yaml` introduced by the automatic pnpm-update commit ([a6df5fe](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED]) on Apr 13 2026) is causing every CI pipeline on `main` to fail. The lockfile importer section for `sdk/postgresql/postgresql-auth` references `vitest@4.1.2` and `@vitest/coverage-istanbul@4.1.2`, but the snapshots section only contains entries for `4.1.4`. All 421 other packages were correctly updated to `4.1.4`. Since every CI pipeline runs `pnpm install` against the shared lockfile, every pipeline fails at the install step.

Inspected CI runs on commit `[REDACTED]` (Apr 14 2026 — the most recent commit with completed CI). **186 CI pipeline jobs** (Build UnitTest + Build Analyze) are failing across 20+ service directories including `notificationhubs`, `search`, `iot`, `template`, `fluidrelay-mgmt`, `applicationinsights-mgmt`, `eventhub-mgmt`, `datafactory-mgmt`, and many more.

## Failures

### All packages (systemic — `pnpm install` fails for the whole workspace)

**Failing tests:** N/A — CI pipelines fail before any tests run.

**Error (from CI annotations):**
```
ERR_PNPM_LOCKFILE_MISSING_DEPENDENCY  Broken lockfile: no entry for
'`@vitest/coverage-istanbul`@4.1.2(vitest@4.1.2)' in pnpm-lock.yaml

This issue is probably caused by a badly resolved merge conflict.
To fix the lockfile, run 'pnpm install --no-frozen-lockfile'.
```

Confirmed locally — running `pnpm install` at the repo root fails immediately with this error.

**Root cause:**

The `pnpm update` PR ([a6df5fe](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/commit/[REDACTED])) bumped `vitest`/`@vitest/coverage-istanbul` from `4.1.2` → `4.1.4` for all 421 workspace packages and updated the snapshots section accordingly (removing `4.1.2` snapshot entries and adding `4.1.4`). However, it failed to update the importer section for `sdk/postgresql/postgresql-auth`, which still references:

- `@vitest/coverage-istanbul`: `version: 4.1.2(vitest@4.1.2)` (no such snapshot exists)
- `vitest`: `version: 4.1.2(`@opentelemetry/api`@1.9.1)(`@types/node`@20.19.39)(`@vitest/browser-playwright`@4.1.2)(vite@7.3.1(...))` (no such snapshot exists)

Because `pnpm install` runs for the entire workspace in every CI pipeline (no per-package filter), this single stale lockfile entry breaks every pipeline.

**Suggested fix:**

```diff
--- a/pnpm-lock.yaml
+++ b/pnpm-lock.yaml
@@ sdk/postgresql/postgresql-auth devDependencies @@
       '`@vitest/coverage-istanbul`':
         specifier: catalog:testing
-        version: 4.1.2(vitest@4.1.2)
+        version: 4.1.4(vitest@4.1.4)
 ...
       vitest:
         specifier: catalog:testing
-        version: 4.1.2(`@opentelemetry/api`@1.9.1)(`@types/node`@20.19.39)(`@vitest/browser-playwright`@4.1.2)(vite@7.3.1(`@types/node`@20.19.39)(terser@5.46.1)(tsx@4.21.0)(yaml@2.8.3))
+        version: 4.1.4(`@opentelemetry/api`@1.9.1)(`@types/node`@20.19.39)(`@vitest/browser-playwright`@4.1.4)(`@vitest/coverage-istanbul`@4.1.4)(vite@7.3.1(`@types/node`@20.19.39)(terser@5.46.1)(tsx@4.21.0)(yaml@2.8.3))
```

The `4.1.4` snapshot entries already exist at lines 41609 and 45097 of the current lockfile, so no new snapshots need to be added.

Alternatively, regenerate the lockfile by running `pnpm install --no-frozen-lockfile` at the repo root (which requires the private Azure DevOps registry to be accessible).

**Reproduced locally:** Yes — `pnpm install` fails with `ERR_PNPM_LOCKFILE_MISSING_DEPENDENCY` on the current `main`.

---

## Additional Notes

- The "After APIView" check runs that appear as failures on the `b9e6755` commit are unrelated — those are GitHub Actions failures for API review on PRs from forked repos and do not represent test failures.
- The CI test-proxy log errors (`Path does not exist: .../test-proxy.log`) are a downstream symptom of the `pnpm install` failure — pipelines fail before the test-proxy is started.
- The broken entry is in the `sdk/postgresql/postgresql-auth` importer section at lines ~23503–23526 of `pnpm-lock.yaml`.

`@azure/ai-content-understanding`

### Issues associated with this PR

- https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/46249 (original report in Python SDK)

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

The REST API returns a `usage` field in the analyze operation status envelope containing billing/metering details (`UsageDetails`), but the JS SDK's `AnalysisResultPoller` did not expose it. Users had no way to access token counts, document page counts, or contextualization token usage after an analyze operation completed.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR chosen?

The approach wraps the existing deserializer with a `deserializeWithUsage` closure that captures `usage` from the operation status envelope before the inner deserializer extracts only the `result` field. The `usage` property is then exposed on the poller via `Object.defineProperty` — the same pattern already used for `operationId`. This is consistent across both `analyze()` and `analyzeBinary()` methods.

Deserialization is guarded with try/catch + `logger.warning()` so a malformed usage field never prevents the user from getting their `AnalysisResult`.

### Are there test cases added in this PR? _(If not, why?)_

Yes — 6 new unit tests in `test/public/node/usage.spec.ts`:
1. Usage from `analyzeBinary` 
2. Usage from `analyze` (URL)
3. `usage` is `undefined` before `pollUntilDone`
4. `usage` is `undefined` when response has no usage field
5. All 7 `UsageDetails` fields deserialized correctly
6. Malformed usage data (e.g. `tokens: 42`) degrades gracefully

### Provide a list of related PRs _(if any)_

- Python: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/pull/46278
- .NET: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/pull/58166

### Command used to generate this PR: **_(Applicable only to SDK release request PRs)_

N/A — manual customization, not generated.

Fixes `TimeoutNegativeWarning` raised by Node.js v24+ when `setTimeout` receives a negative timeout value.

#38163

## Root Cause

In `sdk/eventhub/event-hubs/src/withAuth.ts`, a deadline pattern computes remaining time as `timeoutAfterStartTime - Date.now()`. If prior steps (opening CBS session, acquiring lock) consume more time than the original timeout budget, this goes **negative**. The negative value flows into `setTimeout()` in `requestResponseLink.sendRequest()` and `lock.acquire()`, triggering:

```
TimeoutNegativeWarning: -1 is a negative number.
Timeout duration was set to 1.
```

This is cosmetic — Node clamps to 1ms and the operation succeeds — but the warning triggers ops alerts.

The same pattern exists in Service Bus's `linkEntity.ts`.

## Fix

Clamp timeout values to `Math.max(..., 0)` at all affected sites (defense in depth):

| File | Sites | Change |
|------|-------|--------|
| `sdk/eventhub/event-hubs/src/withAuth.ts` | 4 | Clamp `timeoutAfterStartTime - Date.now()` |
| `sdk/servicebus/service-bus/src/core/linkEntity.ts` | 3 | Clamp `timeoutInMs - (Date.now() - startTime)` |
| `sdk/core/core-amqp/src/requestResponseLink.ts` | 1 | Clamp `timeoutInMs` in `sendRequest()` |
| `sdk/core/core-amqp/src/util/lock.ts` | 1 | Clamp `timeoutInMs` in `acquire()`'s `setTimeout` |","state":"closed","draft":false,"merged":false,"html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/pull/38166","user":{"login":"deyaaeldeen","id":6074665,"profile_url":"https://github.com/deyaaeldeen","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6074665?v=4"},"labels":["Event Hubs","Azure.Core"],"requested_reviewers":["xirzec","axisc","hmlam","sjkwak","j7nw4r","EldertGrootenboer","skarri-microsoft"],"head":{"ref":"fix/negative-timeout-warning","sha":"[REDACTED]","repo":{"full_name":"Azure/azure-sdk-for-js","description":"This repository is for active development of the Azure SDK for JavaScript (NodeJS & Browser). Restructures the breaking-change agent to prevent it from silently skipping the guidelines file during analysis.

### Changes

**Agent file (agent.md)**:
- Mandatory Execution Protocol -- forces reading guidelines and restating workflow phases before analysis
- Execution Phases skeleton -- 5-phase workflow inlined as in-context control flow
- Explicit State Tracking -- phase-appropriate outputs (context, changelog_entries, broken_models, etc.)
- Cascade Detection Checklist -- 4-step checklist for every 'new signature' entry, with naming-trap warning
- Mandatory Pattern Matching Step -- Type 2 entries only, with required output format
- Validation Rules -- concise references to structured sections
- Removed redundant Quick-Reference Checklist (covered by new sections)

**Guidelines file (guidelines.md)**:
- Removed redundant 'consult patterns after investigation' from intro and Phase 4 (agent.md now owns this)

**Patterns file (patterns.md)**: No content changes

**Cross-references**: Updated all internal links for the mgmt- file rename.

### Motivation

The agent frequently skipped the guidelines file because the original agent.md only had a non-binding reference. This restructuring makes agent.md a control plane with mandatory execution steps, while guidelines.md remains the knowledge base.","state":"closed","draft":false,"merged":false,"html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/pull/38244","user":{"login":"JialinHuang803","id":139532647,"profile_url":"https://github.com/JialinHuang803","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/139532647?v=4"},"requested_reviewers":["xirzec","jeremymeng"],"head":{"ref":"improve-breaking-change-agent","sha":"[REDACTED]","repo":{"full_name":"JialinHuang803/azure-sdk-for-js","description":"This repository is for active development of the Azure SDK for JavaScript (NodeJS & Browser). - `@azure/core-amqp`
- `@azure/event-hubs` (tests only)

### Issues associated with this PR

- Fixes #38231
- Supersedes #38190

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

Node.js 20+ defaults to Happy Eyeballs (RFC 8305), racing IPv4/IPv6 connections. DNS failures now throw `AggregateError` instead of a single system error. `translate()` in core-amqp didn't handle this, returning raw untranslated errors — breaking `instanceof MessagingError` checks, `.retryable` logic, and error code handling for all consumers.

### Fix

`translate()` now maps `AggregateError` → new `AggregateError` with all inner errors translated to `MessagingError`, with `.retryable` stamped on the aggregate (true if any inner is retryable).

### Are there test cases added in this PR?

Yes — 6 new tests in `core-amqp` covering ENOTFOUND, EAI_AGAIN, mixed retryability, nested aggregates, and empty aggregates. Event Hubs test helper updated to unwrap `AggregateError`.","state":"closed","draft":false,"merged":false,"html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/pull/38233","user":{"login":"deyaaeldeen","id":6074665,"profile_url":"https://github.com/deyaaeldeen","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6074665?v=4"},"labels":["Azure.Core"],"requested_reviewers":["xirzec","axisc","hmlam","sjkwak","j7nw4r"],"head":{"ref":"fix/core-amqp-aggregate-error-translate","sha":"[REDACTED]","repo":{"full_name":"Azure/azure-sdk-for-js","description":"This repository is for active development of the Azure SDK for JavaScript (NodeJS & Browser). N/A (documentation only)

### Issues associated with this PR

From Agentic Workflow https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/issues/37982

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

Several documentation files in the `documentation/` folder contained inconsistencies with current repo tooling and `@azure/identity` behavior.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR chosen?

Documentation-only changes; no alternative designs applicable.

### Are there test cases added in this PR? _(If not, why?)_

No — these are documentation-only changes.

### Provide a list of related PRs _(if any)_

N/A

---

**Changes made:**

- Use `pnpm build --filter` (without `turbo`) in multiple docs — both `pnpm build` and `pnpm turbo build` resolve the same way in this repo (the `build` script in the root `package.json` delegates to Turbo), but `pnpm build` is shorter and equally correct: `linting.md`, `Quickstart-on-how-to-write-tests.md`, `steps-after-generations.md`, `RLC-Swagger-quickstart.md`, `writing-performance-tests.md`

- Fix `npm install -g @pnpm` → `npm install -g pnpm` in `Generate-code-from-TypeSpec.md` (the `@pnpm` scoped name does not exist on npm)

- Update `DefaultAzureCredential` chain in `using-azure-identity.md` to list all 7 credential types it currently attempts (was listing only 2: `EnvironmentCredential` and `ManagedIdentityCredential`)

- Update `InteractiveBrowserCredential` section in `using-azure-identity.md`:
  - Replace outdated 'implicit grant flow' reference with the current 'authorization code flow'
  - Remove outdated NOTE claiming the credential is browser-only; it has supported Node.js for several releases

### Checklists

- `@azure/event-hubs` (tests only)

### Issues associated with this PR

- CI failures in Event Hubs internal client tests when connection failures surface as:
  - `AggregateError` from `@azure/core-amqp` retry, and
  - transient Node DNS errors (`EAI_AGAIN`) instead of only `ENOTFOUND`.

Addresses https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/issues/38231

### Describe the problem that is addressed by this PR

`validateConnectionError()` in `sdk/eventhub/event-hubs/test/internal/client.spec.ts` was too strict in two ways:

1. It assumed a direct `MessagingError` and did not handle `AggregateError` with nested `MessagingError`s.
2. On Node-like runtimes, it expected only `ENOTFOUND`, but CI can legitimately return `EAI_AGAIN` for transient DNS lookup failures.

This PR updates the helper to:
- unwrap and validate `MessagingError` from either direct rejection or `AggregateError.errors` (preferring the last `MessagingError`), and
- on Node-like runtimes, accept either `ENOTFOUND` or `EAI_AGAIN`.
- on non-Node runtimes, continue expecting `ServiceCommunicationError`.

Also includes a lint-safe assertion update (`assert.fail(...)`) to satisfy `no-unused-expressions`.

### What are the possible designs available to address the problem? If there are more than one possible design, why was the one in this PR chosen?

- **Option A (chosen):** make the test helper resilient to both retry aggregation shape and platform-specific DNS transient error codes.
- **Option B:** alter runtime/library behavior to normalize all failures into a single fixed error code/shape before tests assert.
- **Option C:** relax assertions broadly (for example, only assert error type/message), reducing precision.

Option A is minimal, scoped to test assertions, preserves existing intent, and aligns with current `core-amqp` retry/runtime behavior without changing product code.

### Are there test cases added in this PR? _(If not, why?)_

No new test cases were added. This is a targeted update to existing internal test assertions so current tests remain valid across observed runtime error shapes/codes.

### Provide a list of related PRs _(if any)_

- None.

### Checklists
- [ ] Added impacted package name to the issue description.
- [ ] Does this PR need any fixes in the SDK Generator?** _(If so, create an Issue in the [Autorest/typescript](https://github.com/Azure/autorest.typescript) repository and link it here.)_
- [ ] Added a changelog (if necessary).







Original prompt

Fix CI failure in Event Hubs tests where connection failures may surface as AggregateError from core-amqp retry.

Context:
- Workflow/job logs show Event Hubs unit tests failing because `validateConnectionError` expects a `MessagingError`, but the thrown error is an `AggregateError` containing multiple retry errors.
- In `sdk/core/core-amqp/src/retry.ts`, if there is more than 1 error collected, it throws `new AggregateError(errors)`.
- The failing assertions are in `sdk/eventhub/event-hubs/test/internal/client.spec.ts` inside `validateConnectionError()`.

Task:
1) Update `sdk/eventhub/event-hubs/test/internal/client.spec.ts` so `validateConnectionError()` accepts either:
   - a direct `MessagingError`, OR
   - an `AggregateError` (with an `errors` array) containing one or more `MessagingError` instances.
2) When an `AggregateError` is received, unwrap a contained `MessagingError` (prefer the last one) and validate:
   - it is a `MessagingError`
   - it has `code` equal to `ENOTFOUND` when `isNodeLike` is true, otherwise `ServiceCommunicationError`.
3) Keep the rest of the tests intact.
4) Run/ensure unit tests for `sdk/eventhub/event-hubs` pass.

References:
- Job URL: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/actions/runs/24560509371/job/71807721981
- retry AggregateError behavior: `sdk/core/core-amqp/src/retry.ts` (throws AggregateError when errors.length > 1)
- failing test file: `sdk/eventhub/event-hubs/test/internal/client.spec.ts`


The following is the prior conversation context from the user's chat exploration (may be truncated):

User: Please find a solution for this failing job. Use the logs, job definition, and any referenced files where the failure occurred. Keep your response focused on the solution and include code suggestions when appropriate.

Assistant: The failing job is caused by **Event Hubs tests expecting a single `MessagingError`, but the underlying retry layer can now throw an `AggregateError` when multiple retry attempts fail**.

### Root cause
In `sdk/core/core-amqp/src/retry.ts`, when an operation fails after retries, the retry helper does this:

- If there was **1** failure → it rethrows that error.
- If there were **multiple** failures → it throws `new AggregateError(errors)`.

See `retry.ts` lines **281–285** (ref `[REDACTED]`):
- https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/blob/[REDACTED]/sdk/core/core-amqp/src/retry.ts#L281-L285

In the Event Hubs tests, `validateConnectionError()` currently hard-requires the thrown value to be a `MessagingError`:

`client.spec.ts` lines **20–26**:
- https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/blob/[REDACTED]/sdk/eventhub/event-hubs/test/internal/client.spec.ts#L20-L26

In CI, the “non existent namespace” connection string (`Endpoint=sb://a;...`) triggers retries; after multiple failures the final thrown error becomes an `AggregateError`, so the assertion fails with:
> expected AggregateError … to be an instance of MessagingError

### Solution (recommended): make the tests accept AggregateError and validate inner MessagingError(s)
Update `validateConnectionError()` to handle both:
- direct `MessagingError`
- `AggregateError` containing one or more `MessagingError` instances (pick the last one, or any that matches the expected code)

Suggested patch (`sdk/eventhub/event-hubs/test/internal/client.spec.ts`):

```ts
import { MessagingError } from "@azure/core-amqp";
import { isNodeLike } from "@azure/core-util";

function isAggregateError(err: any): err is AggregateError {
  return err && err.name === "AggregateError" && Array.isArray((err as any).errors);
}

function unwrapMessagingError(err: any): any {
  if (err instanceof MessagingError) {
    return err;
  }

  if (isAggregateError(err)) {
    // Prefer the last MessagingError (often the most relevant/final attempt)
    const messagingErrors = (err as any).errors.filter((e: any) => e instanceof MessagingError);
    if (messagingErrors.length > 0) {
      return messagingErrors[messagingErrors.length - 1];
    }
  }

  return err;
}

async function validateConnectionError(promise: Promise): Promise {
  await expect(promise).to.be.rejected.then((err) => {
    const unwrapped = unwrapMessagingError(err);

    expect(unwrapped)
      .to.be.an.instanceOf(MessagingError)
      .and.has.property("code", isNodeLike ? "ENOTFOUND" : "ServiceCommunicationError");

    return err;
  });
}
```

This keeps the intent of the test (“the operation fails for a DNS/nonexistent namespace reason”) while making it resilient to the retry layer returning an `AggregateError`.

### Alternative (more behavior-changing): unwrap AggregateError in the library
If you strongly want the public API to *never* surface `AggregateError`, you could catch and unwrap inside Event Hubs client operations (e.g., in the code paths for `getEventHubProperties()`, `getPartitionIds()`, subscribe flow, etc.) and rethrow a single `Messa...
